inline_review:
commit 18d59d1cf15605c157261d20385d9149b3ba59e1
Author: Sechang Lim <rhkrqnwk98@gmail.com>

mm/vma: fix memory leak in __mmap_region()

This commit adds an fput() in the abort_munmap error path of __mmap_region()
to clean up a swapped file if __mmap_new_vma() fails.

> diff --git a/mm/vma.c b/mm/vma.c
> index cc382217f730..e88df7fe035a 100644
> --- a/mm/vma.c
> +++ b/mm/vma.c
> @@ -2857,6 +2857,13 @@ static unsigned long __mmap_region(struct file *file, unsigned long addr,
>  	if (map.charged)
>  		vm_unacct_memory(map.charged);
>  abort_munmap:
> +	/*
> +	 * This indicates that .mmap_prepare has set a new file, differing from
> +	 * desc->vm_file. But since we're aborting the operation, only the
> +	 * original file will be cleaned up. Ensure we clean up both.
> +	 */
> +	if (map.file_doesnt_need_get)
> +		fput(map.file);

If mmap_file() fails inside __mmap_new_file_vma(), the error path there
calls fput(vma->vm_file) to release the reference.

When the error propagates up to here and we jump to abort_munmap, will
this fput(map.file) execute a second time on the exact same file object
and cause a double-free?

Also, if call_action_prepare() fails inside call_mmap_prepare() after
vfs_mmap_prepare() has successfully swapped desc->vm_file, it returns early.
Because of this early return, map.file_doesnt_need_get is never set to true.

When the error propagates here, will the newly added fput(map.file)
cleanup be skipped, permanently leaking the swapped file?

Finally, if the mapping successfully merges with an adjacent VMA,
__mmap_new_vma() is bypassed entirely.

Since __mmap_region() subsequently returns success without ever calling
fput(map.file), does this permanently leak the additional file reference
acquired by .mmap_prepare()?

>  	vms_abort_munmap_vmas(&map.vms, &map.mas_detach);
>  	return error;
>  }

commit 18d59d1cf15605c157261d20385d9149b3ba59e1
Author: Sechang Lim <rhkrqnwk98@gmail.com>
Date:   Tue Mar 31 18:08:11 2026 +0000

    mm/vma: fix memory leak in __mmap_region()
    
    commit 605f6586ecf7 ("mm/vma: do not leak memory when .mmap_prepare
    swaps the file") handled the success path by skipping get_file() via
    file_doesnt_need_get, but missed the error path.
    
    When /dev/zero is mmap'd with MAP_SHARED, mmap_zero_prepare() calls
    shmem_zero_setup_desc() which allocates a new shmem file to back the
    mapping. If __mmap_new_vma() subsequently fails, this replacement
    file is never fput()'d - the original is released by
    ksys_mmap_pgoff(), but nobody releases the new one.
    
    Add fput() for the swapped file in the error path.
    
    Reproducible with fault injection.
    
    FAULT_INJECTION: forcing a failure.
    name failslab, interval 1, probability 0, space 0, times 1

    Fixes: 605f6586ecf7 ("mm/vma: do not leak memory when .mmap_prepare swaps the file")
    Reviewed-by: Lorenzo Stoakes (Oracle) <ljs@kernel.org>
    Signed-off-by: Sechang Lim <rhkrqnwk98@gmail.com>
    -------
    v2:
     - Drop redundant map.file NULL check (Lorenzo)
     - Add comment explaining the fput() (Lorenzo)
    v1: https://lore.kernel.org/linux-mm/20260331121906.1301155-1-rhkrqnwk98@gmail.com/
     mm/vma.c | 7 +++++++
     1 file changed, 7 insertions(+)

diff --git a/mm/vma.c b/mm/vma.c
index cc382217f730..e88df7fe035a 100644
--- a/mm/vma.c
+++ b/mm/vma.c
@@ -2857,6 +2857,13 @@ static unsigned long __mmap_region(struct file *file, unsigned long addr,
 	if (map.charged)
 		vm_unacct_memory(map.charged);
 abort_munmap:
+	/*
+	 * This indicates that .mmap_prepare has set a new file, differing from
+	 * desc->vm_file. But since we're aborting the operation, only the
+	 * original file will be cleaned up. Ensure we clean up both.
+	 */
+	if (map.file_doesnt_need_get)
+		fput(map.file);
 	vms_abort_munmap_vmas(&map.vms, &map.mas_detach);
 	return error;
 }

Let's look more closely.  `mmap_zero_prepare` itself calls `shmem_zero_setup_desc` if the vma is shared. That calls `__shmem_zero_setup`, which allocates a new file and puts it in `desc->vm_file`. So `vfs_mmap_prepare` succeeds, returning 0 and setting up the new file. Then `call_mmap_prepare` proceeds to `call_action_prepare()`. Meaning, the *newly* allocated file in `desc->vm_file` will not be fput! That's the leak.

I reviewed `call_mmap_prepare` and it confirms this. If `call_action_prepare` fails, the `err` is returned, and back to `abort_munmap` we go. The original file is in `map->file`, and `map->file_doesnt_need_get` is `false`. So `__mmap_region` jumps to `abort_munmap`, but the new file that was allocated by the driver in `desc->vm_file` is leaked because the local variable `desc` is destroyed.

And then I realized: if `call_action_prepare` *succeeds*, we *do* set `map->file = desc->vm_file` and `map->file_doesnt_need_get = true`. But what if `__mmap_region` fails *later*?     "thought": "**Analyzing the mmap/fput Logic in the Zero-Page Mapping Scenario**

Okay, so the core issue is that when we're mmap'ing `/dev/zero` with `MAP_SHARED`, a new shmem file is created, and if the subsequent VMA creation (`__mmap_new_vma`) fails, we could leak this new file. The original patch addresses this. Let's think this through...

First, I see the patch adds a check to `abort_munmap` to `fput` the file: `if (map.file_doesnt_need_get) fput(map.file);`. Right, this makes sense *if* `call_mmap_prepare` has modified `map.file`. If `.mmap_prepare` (aka `shmem_zero_setup_desc`) does replace the file, then `map.file` points to the *new* file. Importantly, `do_mmap` or `ksys_mmap_pgoff` still hold the *original* file's reference via `fget()`. And if `__mmap_region` succeeds, the new VMA will take ownership of the new file (by getting `vma->vm_file = map.file`).     "thought": "**My Analysis of a Potential Memory Mapping Vulnerability**

Okay, so I'm following the `do_mmap` call, which in turn hits `mmap_region`. That then branches down to `__mmap_region`. The typical flow is that `__mmap_region` returns the allocated address, or, in case of failure, an error pointer disguised as an `unsigned long`, like `-ENOMEM`.

Now, here's where it gets interesting, and concerning. Inside `__mmap_region`, there's a conditional block:

```c
	if (have_mmap_prepare && allocated_new) {
		error = mmap_action_complete(vma, &desc.action);
		if (error)
			return error;
	}
```

If `mmap_action_complete` fails, `__mmap_region` simply *returns* that error code. The problem is, and this just hit me, the VMA (Virtual Memory Area) is *already* allocated, merged, and inserted into the Maple Tree *before* this check! So, if `mmap_action_complete` fails, and `__mmap_region` returns the error, `do_mmap` propagates that error back to the caller, effectively telling userspace the `mmap` failed.     "thought": "**Analysis of a Potential Memory Leak in `__mmap_region` and Proposed Fix**

Alright, let's break this down. My initial thought process has led me to identify a critical memory leak vulnerability within the `__mmap_region` function, specifically related to how file references are handled during `mmap` operations when `vfs_mmap_prepare` swaps the file, and `call_action_prepare` then fails.

Here's the gist: `mmap_action_finish()` calls `do_munmap()` in case `mmap_action_complete()` fails. Consequently, the VMA is unmapped, and `do_munmap()` drops the file reference, which is `vma->vm_file`. This is not a leak. Also, `mmap_action_complete()` is only called if a *new* VMA is allocated.

When a VMA merges with an existing VMA, `mmap_action_complete` is skipped.

There are four potential failure paths in `__mmap_region`:

1.  `__mmap_setup` fails. No leak.
2.  `call_mmap_prepare` fails. **This is where the leak exists.**
3.  `__mmap_new_vma` fails. The fix works.
4.  `mmap_action_complete` fails. No leak.

The only true leak stems from path 2: `call_mmap_prepare()` fails because `call_action_prepare()` returns an error, *after* `vfs_mmap_prepare()` has successfully swapped `desc->vm_file`. This swapped file has a refcount of 1 and is leaked if `call_action_prepare()` fails.

Let's delve into the specifics of this scenario. When `call_mmap_prepare()` gets called, `vfs_mmap_prepare` runs. If `vfs_mmap_prepare` succeeds, it might swap `desc->vm_file` for a *new* file. Next, `call_action_prepare` is invoked. If `call_action_prepare` fails, the function returns the error *without* updating `map->file_doesnt_need_get` or `map->file`. In other words, `map->file_doesnt_need_get` remains false and the incorrect file may be referenced, and thus, the new file allocated via `desc->vm_file` is leaked. The kernel then jumps to `abort_munmap`. Because we did not set `map->file_doesnt_need_get` to `true`, `fput(map.file)` is *not* called, resulting in a leak of the *new* file.

This is fundamentally due to a flaw in how `call_mmap_prepare` handles the swapped file refcount in this failure scenario.  The subsequent commit by the patch author aims to fix a leak in path 3, but *completely misses* this critical path 2, where the *new* file becomes orphaned after `vfs_mmap_prepare` is run, but `call_action_prepare()` fails. The fix in the commit would only work if the subsequent error happened after `call_mmap_prepare()` has updated these fields.

My proposed fix, and a *general fix* for both path 2 and path 3, is to introduce the following:

```c
	if (desc.vm_file != file)
		fput(desc.vm_file);
```

within the `abort_munmap` function. This elegantly covers *both* scenarios, regardless of whether `call_action_prepare` or `__mmap_new_vma` fails.  I propose to modify `call_mmap_prepare` as such:

```c
static int call_mmap_prepare(struct mmap_state *map,
		struct vm_area_desc *desc)
{
	int err;

	/* Invoke the hook. */
	err = vfs_mmap_prepare(map->file, desc);
	if (err)
		return err;

	/* Update file immediately if swapped, so caller can clean up */
	if (desc->vm_file != map->file) {
		map->file_doesnt_need_get = true;
		map->file = desc->vm_file;
	}

	err = call_action_prepare(map, desc);
	if (err)
		return err;

	/* Update fields permitted to be changed. */
	map->pgoff = desc->pgoff;
	map->vma_flags = desc->vma_flags;

    "content": "{"content":"// SPDX-License-Identifier: GPL-2.0-or-later\n\n/*\n * VMA-specific functions.\n */\n\n#include \"vma_internal.h\"\n#include \"vma.h\"\n\nstruct mmap_state {\n\tstruct mm_struct *mm;\n\tstruct vma_iterator *vmi;\n\n\tunsigned long addr;\n\tunsigned long end;\n\tpgoff_t pgoff;\n\tunsigned long pglen;\n\tunion {\n\t\tvm_flags_t vm_flags;\n\t\tvma_flags_t vma_flags;\n\t};\n\tstruct file *file;\n\tpgprot_t page_prot;\n\n\t/* User-defined fields, perhaps updated by .mmap_prepare(). */\n\tconst struct vm_operations_struct *vm_ops;\n\tvoid *vm_private_data;\n\n\tunsigned long charged;\n\n\tstruct vm_area_struct *prev;\n\tstruct vm_area_struct *next;\n\n\t/* Unmapping state. */\n\tstruct vma_munmap_struct vms;\n\tstruct ma_state mas_detach;\n\tstruct maple_tree mt_detach;\n\n\t/* Determine if we can check KSM flags early in mmap() logic. */\n\tbool check_ksm_early :1;\n\t/* If .mmap_prepare changed the file, we don't need to pin. */\n\tbool file_doesnt_need_get :1;\n};\n\n#define MMAP_STATE(name, mm_, vmi_, addr_, len_, pgoff_, vma_flags_, file_) \\\n\tstruct mmap_state name = {\t\t\t\t\t\\\n\t\t.mm = mm_,\t\t\t\t\t\t\\\n\t\t.vmi = vmi_,\t\t\t\t\t\t\\\n\t\t.addr = addr_,\t\t\t\t\t\t\\\n\t\t.end = (addr_) + (len_),\t\t\t\t\\\n\t\t.pgoff = pgoff_,\t\t\t\t\t\\\n\t\t.pglen = PHYS_PFN(len_),\t\t\t\t\\\n\t\t.vma_flags = vma_flags_,\t\t\t\t\\\n\t\t.file = file_,\t\t\t\t\t\t\\\n\t\t.page_prot = vma_get_page_prot(vma_flags_),\t\t\\\n\t}\n\n#define VMG_MMAP_STATE(name, map_, vma_)\t\t\t\t\\\n\tstruct vma_merge_struct name = {\t\t\t\t\\\n\t\t.mm = (map_)->mm,\t\t\t\t\t\\\n\t\t.vmi = (map_)->vmi,\t\t\t\t\t\\\n\t\t.start = (map_)->addr,\t\t\t\t\t\\\n\t\t.end = (map_)->end,\t\t\t\t\t\\\n\t\t.vma_flags = (map_)->vma_flags,\t\t\t\t\\\n\t\t.pgoff = (map_)->pgoff,\t\t\t\t\t\\\n\t\t.file = (map_)->file,\t\t\t\t\t\\\n\t\t.prev = (map_)->prev,\t\t\t\t\t\\\n\t\t.middle = vma_,\t\t\t\t\t\t\\\n\t\t.next = (vma_) ? NULL : (map_)->next,\t\t\t\\\n\t\t.state = VMA_MERGE_START,\t\t\t\t\\\n\t}\n\n/* Was this VMA ever forked from a parent, i.e. maybe contains CoW mappings? */\nstatic bool vma_is_fork_child(struct vm_area_struct *vma)\n{\n\t/*\n\t * The list_is_singular() test is to avoid merging VMA cloned from\n\t * parents. This can improve scalability caused by the anon_vma root\n\t * lock.\n\t */\n\treturn vma && vma->anon_vma && !list_is_singular(&vma->anon_vma_chain);\n}\n\nstatic inline bool is_mergeable_vma(struct vma_merge_struct *vmg, bool merge_next)\n{\n\tstruct vm_area_struct *vma = merge_next ? vmg->next : vmg->prev;\n\tvma_flags_t diff;\n\n\tif (!mpol_equal(vmg->policy, vma_policy(vma)))\n\t\treturn false;\n\n\tdiff = vma_flags_diff_pair(&vma->flags, &vmg->vma_flags);\n\tvma_flags_clear_mask(&diff, VMA_IGNORE_MERGE_FLAGS);\n\n\tif (!vma_flags_empty(&diff))\n\t\treturn false;\n\tif (vma->vm_file != vmg->file)\n\t\treturn false;\n\tif (!is_mergeable_vm_userfaultfd_ctx(vma, vmg->uffd_ctx))\n\t\treturn false;\n\tif (!anon_vma_name_eq(anon_vma_name(vma), vmg->anon_name))\n\t\treturn false;\n\treturn true;\n}\n\nstatic bool is_mergeable_anon_vma(struct vma_merge_struct *vmg, bool merge_next)\n{\n\tstruct vm_area_struct *tgt = merge_next ? vmg->next : vmg->prev;\n\tstruct vm_area_struct *src = vmg->middle; /* existing merge case. */\n\tstruct anon_vma *tgt_anon = tgt->anon_vma;\n\tstruct anon_vma *src_anon = vmg->anon_vma;\n\n\t/*\n\t * We _can_ have !src, vmg->anon_vma via copy_vma(). In this instance we\n\t * will remove the existing VMA's anon_vma's so there's no scalability\n\t * concerns.\n\t */\n\tVM_WARN_ON(src && src_anon != src->anon_vma);\n\n\t/* Case 1 - we will dup_anon_vma() from src into tgt. */\n\tif (!tgt_anon && src_anon) {\n\t\tstruct vm_area_struct *copied_from = vmg->copied_from;\n\n\t\tif (vma_is_fork_child(src))\n\t\t\treturn false;\n\t\tif (vma_is_fork_child(copied_from))\n\t\t\treturn false;\n\n\t\treturn true;\n\t}\n\t/* Case 2 - we will simply use tgt's anon_vma. */\n\tif (tgt_anon && !src_anon)\n\t\treturn !vma_is_fork_child(tgt);\n\t/* Case 3 - the anon_vma's are already shared. */\n\treturn src_anon == tgt_anon;\n}\n\n/*\n * init_multi_vma_prep() - Initializer for struct vma_prepare\n * @vp: The vma_prepare struct\n * @vma: The vma that will be altered once locked\n * @vmg: The merge state that will be used to determine adjustment and VMA\n *       removal.\n */\nstatic void &vp->remove;\n\n\tmemset(vp, 0, sizeof(struct vma_prepare));\n\tvp->vma = vma;\n\tvp->anon_vma = vma->anon_vma;\n\n\tif (vmg && vmg->__remove_middle) {\n\t\t*remove = vmg->middle;\n\t\tremove = &vp->remove2;\n\t}\n\tif (vmg && vmg->__remove_next)\n\t\t*remove = vmg->next;\n\n\tif (vmg && vmg->__adjust_middle_start)\n\t\tadjust = vmg->middle;\n\telse if (vmg && vmg->__adjust_next_start)\n\t\tadjust = vmg->next;\n\telse\n\t\tadjust = NULL;\n\n\tvp->adj_next = adjust;\n\tif (!vp->anon_vma && adjust)\n\t\tvp->anon_vma = adjust->anon_vma;\n\n\tVM_WARN_ON(vp->anon_vma && adjust && adjust->anon_vma &&\n\t\t   vp->anon_vma != adjust->anon_vma);\n\n\tvp->file = vma->vm_file;\n\tif (vp->file)\n\t\tvp->mapping = vma->vm_file->f_mapping;\n\n\tif (vmg && vmg->skip_vma_uprobe)\n\t\tvp->skip_vma_uprobe = true;\n}\n\n/*\n * Return true if we can merge this (vma_flags,anon_vma,file,vm_pgoff)\n * in front of (at a lower virtual address and file offset than) the vma.\n *\n * We cannot merge two vmas if they have differently assigned (non-NULL)\n * anon_vmas, nor if same anon_vma is assigned but offsets incompatible.\n *\n * We don't check here for the merged mmap wrapping around the end of pagecache\n * indices (16TB on ia32) because do_mmap() does not permit mmap's which\n * wrap, nor mmaps which cover the final page at index -1UL.\n *\n * We assume the vma may be removed as part of the merge.\n */\nstatic bool can_vma_merge_before(struct vma_merge_struct *vmg)\n{\n\tpgoff_t pglen = PHYS_PFN(vmg->end - vmg->start);\n\n\tif (is_mergeable_vma(vmg, /* merge_next = */ true) &&\n\t    is_mergeable_anon_vma(vmg, /* merge_next = */ true)) {\n\t\tif (vmg->next->vm_pgoff == vmg->pgoff + pglen)\n\t\t\treturn true;\n\t}\n\n\treturn false;\n}\n\n/*\n * Return true if we can merge this (vma_flags,anon_vma,file,vm_pgoff)\n * beyond (at a higher virtual address and file offset than) the vma.\n *\n * We cannot merge two vmas if they have differently assigned (non-NULL)\n * anon_vmas, nor if same anon_vma is assigned but offsets incompatible.\n *\n * We assume that vma is not removed as part of the merge.\n */\nstatic bool can_vma_merge_after(struct vma_merge_struct *vmg)\n{\n\tif (is_mergeable_vma(vmg, /* merge_next = */ false) &&\n\t    is_mergeable_anon_vma(vmg, /* merge_next = */ false)) {\n\t\tif (vmg->prev->vm_pgoff + vma_pages(vmg->prev) == vmg->pgoff)\n\t\t\treturn true;\n\t}\n\treturn false;\n}\n\nstatic void __vma_link_file(struct vm_area_struct *vma,\n\t\t\t    struct address_space *mapping)\n{\n\tif (vma_is_shared_maywrite(vma))\n\t\tmapping_allow_writable(mapping);\n\n\tflush_dcache_mmap_lock(mapping);\n\tvma_interval_tree_insert(vma, &mapping->i_mmap);\n\tflush_dcache_mmap_unlock(mapping);\n}\n\n/*\n * Requires inode->i_mapping->i_mmap_rwsem\n */\nstatic void __remove_shared_vm_struct(struct vm_area_struct *vma,\n\t\t\t\t      struct address_space *mapping)\n{\n\tif (vma_is_shared_maywrite(vma))\n\t\tmapping_unmap_writable(mapping);\n\n\tflush_dcache_mmap_lock(mapping);\n\tvma_interval_tree_remove(vma, &mapping->i_mmap);\n\tflush_dcache_mmap_unlock(mapping);\n}\n\n/*\n * vma has some anon_vma assigned, and is already inserted on that\n * anon_vma's interval trees.\n *\n * Before updating the vma's vm_start / vm_end / vm_pgoff fields, the\n * vma must be removed from the anon_vma's interval trees using\n * anon_vma_interval_tree_pre_update_vma().\n *\n * After the update, the vma will be reinserted using\n * anon_vma_interval_tree_post_update_vma().\n *\n * The entire update must be protected by exclusive mmap_lock and by\n * the root anon_vma's mutex.\n */\nstatic *avc;\n\n\tlist_for_each_entry(avc, &vma->anon_vma_chain, same_vma)\n\t\tanon_vma_interval_tree_insert(avc, &avc->anon_vma->rb_root);\n}\n\n/*\n * vma_prepare() - Helper function for handling locking VMAs prior to altering\n * @vp: The initialized vma_prepare struct\n */\nstatic void vma_prepare(struct vma_prepare *vp)\n{\n\tif (vp->file) {\n\t\tuprobe_munmap(vp->vma, vp->vma->vm_start, && vp->insert->vm_file) {\n\t\t\t/*\n\t\t\t * Put into interval tree now, so instantiated pages\n\t\t\t * are visible to arm/parisc __flush_dcache_page\n\t\t\t * throughout; but we cannot insert into address\n\t\t\t * space until vma start or end is updated.\n\t\t\t */\n\t\t\t__vma_link_file(vp->insert,\n\t\t\t\t\tvp->insert->vm_file->f_mapping);\n\t\t}\n\t}\n\n\tif (vp->anon_vma) {\n\t\tanon_vma_lock_write(vp->anon_vma);\n\t\tanon_vma_interval_tree_pre_update_vma(vp->vma);\n\t\tif (vp->adj_next)\n\t\t\tanon_vma_interval_tree_pre_update_vma(vp->adj_next);\n\t}\n\n\tif The first step is the minimum needed to fulfill the start\n\t * gap, the next step is the minimum to align that. It is the minimum\n\t * needed to fulfill both.\n\t */\n\tgap = vma_iter_addr(&vmi) + info->start_gap;\n\tgap += (info->align_offset - gap) & info->align_mask;\n\ttmp = vma_next(&vmi);\n\t/* Avoid prev check if possible */\n\tif (tmp && vma_test_any_mask(tmp, VMA_STARTGAP_FLAGS)) {\n\t\tif (vm_start_gap(tmp) < gap + length - 1) {\n\t\t\tlow_limit = tmp->vm_end;\n\t\t\tvma_iter_reset(&vmi);\n\t\t\tgoto retry;\n\t\t}\n\t} else {\n\t\ttmp = vma_prev(&vmi);\n\t\tif (tmp && vm_end_gap(tmp) > gap) {\n\t\t\tlow_limit = vm_end_gap(tmp);\n\t\t\tvma_iter_reset(&vmi);\n\t\t\tgoto retry;\n\t\t}\n\t}\n\n\treturn gap;\n}\n\n/**\n * unmapped_area_topdown() - Find an area between the low_limit and the\n * high_limit with the correct alignment and offset at the highest available\n * address, all from @info. Note: current->mm is used for the search.\n *\n * @info: The unmapped area information including the range [low_limit -\n * high_limit), the alignment offset and mask.\n *\n * Return: A memory address or -ENOMEM.\n */\nunsigned long unmapped_area_topdown(struct vm_unmapped_area_info *info)\n{\n\tunsigned long length, gap, gap_end;\n\tunsigned long low_limit, high_limit;\n\tstruct vm_area_struct *tmp;\n\tVMA_ITERATOR(vmi, current->mm, 0);\n\n\t/* Adjust search length to account for worst case alignment overhead */\n\tlength = info->length + info->align_mask + info->start_gap;\n\tif (length < info->length)\n\t\treturn -ENOMEM;\n\n\tlow_limit = info->low_limit;\n\tif (low_limit < mmap_min_addr)\n\t\tlow_limit = mmap_min_addr;\n\thigh_limit = info->high_limit;\nretry:\n\tif (vma_iter_area_highest(&vmi, low_limit, high_limit, length))\n\t\treturn -ENOMEM;\n\n\tgap = vma_iter_end(&vmi) - info->length;\n\tgap -= (gap - info->align_offset) & info->align_mask;\n\tgap_end = vma_iter_end(&vmi);\n\ttmp = vma_next(&vmi);\n\t /* Avoid prev check if possible */\n\tif (tmp && vma_test_any_mask(tmp, VMA_STARTGAP_FLAGS)) {\n\t\tif (vm_start_gap(tmp) < gap_end) {\n\t\t\thigh_limit = vm_start_gap(tmp);\n\t\t\tvma_iter_reset(&vmi);\n\t\t\tgoto retry;\n\t\t}\n\t} else {\n\t\ttmp = vma_prev(&vmi);\n\t\tif (tmp && vm_end_gap(tmp) > gap) {\n\t\t\thigh_limit = tmp->vm_start;\n\t\t\tvma_iter_reset(&vmi);\n\t\t\tgoto retry;\n\t\t}\n\t}\n\n\treturn gap;\n}\n\n/*\n * Verify that the stack growth is acceptable and\n * update accounting. This is shared with both the\n * grow-up and grow-down cases.\n */\nstatic int acct_stack_growth(struct vm_area_struct *vma,\n\t\t\t     unsigned long size, unsigned long grow)\n{\n\tstruct mm_struct *mm = vma->vm_mm;\n\tunsigned long new_start;\n\n\t/* address space limit tests */\n\tif (!may_expand_vm(mm, &vma->flags, grow))\n\t\treturn -ENOMEM;\n\n\t/* Stack limit test */\n\tif (size > rlimit(RLIMIT_STACK))\n\t\treturn -ENOMEM;\n\n\t/* mlock limit tests */\n\tif (!mlock_future_ok(mm, vma_test(vma, VMA_LOCKED_BIT),\n\t\t\t     grow << PAGE_SHIFT))\n\t\treturn -ENOMEM;\n\n\t/* Check to ensure the stack will not grow into a hugetlb-only region */\n\tnew_start = vma->vm_end - size;\n#ifdef CONFIG_STACK_GROWSUP\n\tif (vma_test(vma, VMA_GROWSUP_BIT))\n\t\tnew_start = vma->vm_start;\n#endif\n\tif (is_hugepage_only_range(vma->vm_mm, new_start, size))\n\t\treturn -EFAULT;\n\n\t/*\n\t * Overcommit.. Okay, let's break this down. It seems the problem lies within how `mmap_action_complete` interacts with the broader mmap process, specifically when it fails. If `mmap_action_complete` fails, it returns an error directly, but critically, the VMA is already visible and potentially linked in the rbtree. This seems unusual, as we typically expect that VMA creation is atomic. A failure here might mean a post-operation failed, but the region is already mapped, which raises an immediate flag.

The key observation is how `__mmap_region` handles errors. If it fails, it returns an error code (casted to unsigned long) and this error will bubble up to `mmap_region()`. The key question is whether a VMA is torn down correctly. The `abort_munmap` section comes into play during error handling, which is triggered when `__mmap_setup` or `call_mmap_prepare` fails. In this scenario, no VMA has been created. However, if `__mmap_new_vma` fails, it jumps to `unacct_error` and then to `abort_munmap`.

Now, if `__mmap_new_file_vma` fails, *it* already calls `fput(vma->vm_file)` which is also `map.file` (due to the file swap). This suggests a possible double free, which is confirmed by inspecting the failure path: After `__mmap_new_file_vma` fails, it calls `fput(map.file)`. Then, control is transferred to `__mmap_region` and eventually `abort_munmap`, where *another* `fput(map.file)` is called, which results in a double free, or use-after-free, on the file object.

Let's trace this back to the origin of the file descriptor. Specifically, the patch introduces a `fput(map.file)` call at `abort_munmap`. This `fput` is problematic in this code path because the `map.file_doesnt_need_get` field is true (due to the `call_mmap_prepare` file swap), and therefore, `map.file` is freed twice, because one is in `__mmap_new_file_vma`, and then again in `abort_munmap`.

We can verify this. If `__mmap_new_file_vma` fails, it does `fput(vma->vm_file)`. And that is the file we care about: `map->file`. If the `mmap_file` call within `__mmap_new_file_vma` fails, `fput` is called to decrement the file reference, and the error code bubbles up. `__mmap_region` catches the error, jumps to `unacct_error`, and eventually to `abort_munmap`, where we see `fput(map.file)` *again*. So this confirms the double `fput()` on the same file pointer.

Now, a confusing point: The commit message says that `__mmap_new_vma()` might leak the file. This is true if `__mmap_new_vma` fails BEFORE calling `__mmap_new_file_vma`. If `vm_area_alloc()`, `vma_start_write_killable()`, or `vma_iter_prealloc()` fail, the file pointer is leaked! Because `mmap_zero_prepare()` allocated it and swapped `map.file`, and nobody frees it. However, if it fails INSIDE `__mmap_new_file_vma()` *that* function does free it, and then `__mmap_region()` will double-free it!

To wrap it up, the root cause is that `__mmap_new_file_vma` may call `fput` under error conditions, but then `abort_munmap` ends up calling fput() *again* on the same file in the case of failures inside `__mmap_new_file_vma`. This double-free is the crux of this bug. It could lead to reference count underflow or use-after-free conditions.

I began to think through several failure scenarios. If `mmap_file` fails, `fput(vma->vm_file)` is called, so the file is freed. But what if the merging of VMAs fails with `vma_merge_new_range`? Nope, that function doesn't touch `fput(map.file)`. It returns `NULL` and `__mmap_new_vma` is invoked.

Then I considered `__mmap_new_vma` failing before `mmap_file` is called. For instance, if `vm_area_alloc` returns `NULL`, then `__mmap_new_vma` returns `-ENOMEM`. Ultimately, `__mmap_region` sees the error and goes to `abort_munmap`, where `fput(map->file)` correctly frees the swapped file!

However, I realized a subtle issue: If the failure happens *after* `mmap_file` fails *inside* `__mmap_new_file_vma`, then `__mmap_new_file_vma` does `fput(vma->vm_file)`, and *then* `__mmap_region` does `fput(map->file)`. That's a double free!

I double-checked that `fput(vma->vm_file)` in `__mmap_new_file_vma` sets `vma->vm_file = NULL`. It does, but `map->file` *still* points to the same file! That's why the `__mmap_region`'s error path will lead to a double `fput()`.

The author introduced a fix for a memory leak that happened during `__mmap_new_vma` failures. The `vm_area_alloc` call failed, and that's when the memory leak occurred. Specifically, the fault injection stack trace showed that `vm_area_alloc` failed (it's the first thing in `__mmap_new_vma`). Since `vm_area_alloc` failed, `__mmap_new_file_vma` was NEVER called. So the file was never `fput()`! The fix, which involved adding `fput(map.file)` to `abort_munmap` in `__mmap_region`, introduced a double-free *if* the error happens *inside* `__mmap_new_file_vma` because `fput` would ALREADY have been called.

If `map->file_doesnt_need_get` is true, it skips `get_file()`. Then `fput()` in the failure path is effectively consuming the swapped reference.

If `get_file()` was skipped, does `__mmap_new_file_vma` "own" the reference? It does if it fails! In the success path, the reference is transferred to the VMA. In the failure path of `vm_area_alloc` (before calling `__mmap_new_file_vma`), the reference was still owned by `map->file`. If `abort_munmap` does `fput(map->file)`, it double frees if `__mmap_new_file_vma` already `fput` it!

    "thought": "**Analysis of Double `fput()` Vulnerability in `mmap` Implementation**

Okay, so I've been diving deep into this `mmap` code, and I think I've got a handle on this double `fput()` issue. Here's what I'm seeing: The core problem stems from a patch that was added to address a file leak when a `.mmap_prepare` hook is used to swap out a file, but the subsequent VMA allocation fails. The patch correctly adds a cleanup path, but it introduces a subtle but critical flaw in the error handling.

Essentially, when a file swap occurs within the `mmap` process (like with `/dev/zero` using `mmap_zero_prepare`), a new file is allocated and the original is, in effect, replaced. The `.mmap_prepare` hook is called, and it sets `map->file_doesnt_need_get = true` in the `call_mmap_prepare` function to indicate that the caller will *not* need to call `get_file` on the new file. This is crucial because it means the new file has a reference count of 1.

The code then tries to allocate a new VMA using `__mmap_new_vma`. If this allocation fails *early* (like in `vm_area_alloc()`, `vma_start_write_killable()`, or `vma_iter_prealloc()`), the patch correctly adds `fput(map.file)` at the `abort_munmap` label, which is the fix. The file is dropped.

However, if `__mmap_new_vma` succeeds *up to* the point of calling `__mmap_new_file_vma`, and then *within* `__mmap_new_file_vma` itself, a failure occurs (specifically within `mmap_file()`), a double free occurs!

Inside `__mmap_new_file_vma`, an error path has `fput(vma->vm_file)`. Since `vma->vm_file` *is* `map->file`, and `map->file_doesnt_need_get` is true (meaning `get_file()` was *not* called), this `fput()` drops the only reference on the shmem file, freeing it.

The error then propagates up to `__mmap_region`. The code executes: `goto unacct_error;` -> `abort_munmap`. And at `abort_munmap`, the patch *again* checks `map.file_doesnt_need_get` and *again* calls `fput(map.file)`. Boom. Second free.

So the double free occurs because the patch blindly `fput()`s the file without regard to whether the failure was *before* `__mmap_new_file_vma` (which needs the cleanup) or *inside* `__mmap_new_file_vma` (which has already done the cleanup). The flag `map->file_doesnt_need_get` is not cleared appropriately to prevent the redundant `fput`. The patch now makes a double free by calling `fput` on an already-freed file.

Here's the summary:
`call_mmap_prepare` calls a function that replaces the file (`mmap_zero_prepare`). It sets `file_doesnt_need_get = true;`.

If `__mmap_new_vma` fails because `vm_area_alloc()` fails (or earlier), the patch adds `fput(map.file)` in the error path. The patch is correct.

If `__mmap_new_vma` succeeds and calls `__mmap_new_file_vma`, but `mmap_file()` fails *inside* `__mmap_new_file_vma()`, the code calls `fput(map->file)` and returns an error. This is a problem. The error is then bubbled up to the `__mmap_region` error label where the patch calls `fput(map->file)` *again*, double freeing the shmem file!

    "thought": "**Analyzing the `__mmap_region` Function During Memory Mapping**

Okay, so I'm looking at the `__mmap_region` function, specifically the part where it's handling potential errors during a memory map operation.  I've got the context:  I see the `MMAP_STATE` macro setting things up, then a call to `__mmap_setup`.  After that call, it's checking for an error, and if there is one, it jumps to `abort_munmap`. That `abort_munmap` section looks crucial for cleaning up.  

The comment there is what catches my eye. It mentions `.mmap_prepare` possibly changing the file descriptor associated with the memory mapping, but we are aborting the operation. The original file will be cleaned up, however the new file set from `.mmap_prepare` will not.

The interesting bit is that `abort_munmap` checks `map.file_doesnt_need_get`. This looks like a flag to indicate whether a `fput` needs to be called on the original file. Here's the breakdown of my thought process:

*   I noticed that `call_mmap_prepare` can update `desc->vm_file`. This new file presumably has an already bumped refcount. The crucial flag is `file_doesnt_need_get`, indicating whether `get_file()` was needed (or skipped) for the new file.
*   If `__mmap_new_vma` fails, we go to `abort_munmap`, which *might* cause a `fput()` on the *original* file.

*   In `abort_munmap`, if `file_doesnt_need_get` is true, we call `fput(map.file)`. Since `map.file` is the same file as `vma->vm_file`, and `fput` was just called on that file in `__mmap_new_file_vma`, the second `fput` is a double-free.
*   I confirmed that the `vma->vm_file` and `map.file` point to the same file. I also verified the `file_doesnt_need_get` flag doesn't get modified in-between.
*   I then tried to understand whether `__mmap_new_vma` might fail after a successful call to `__mmap_new_file_vma`. It's possible for there to be a failure with `__mmap_new_vma`, but it seems to happen *before* the critical call to `__mmap_new_file_vma`.
*   Finally, I considered the `vma_merge_new_range` function. If merging succeeds, a new `vma` isn't created, and `__mmap_new_vma` is skipped. This seems to be a safe path.

In summary, I've identified a race condition. If `mmap_file` fails after `__mmap_new_file_vma`, then the code will `fput()` the same file twice, leading to a double-free or other memory corruption issues.

If `f_op->mmap_prepare` is used, it looks like `f_op->mmap` might be bypassed. Interesting.

I traced the `have_mmap_prepare` path, and it leads to `call_mmap_prepare` and then `vma_merge_new_range`. If `vma_merge_new_range` succeeds, `allocated_new` is false. This is a crucial point. Now, if `allocated_new` is true, it calls `mmap_action_complete`. But what happens to the file reference if the merge is successful?

However, `map->file` *could* have been replaced during `call_mmap_prepare` (`desc->vm_file != map->file`). This indicates a *new* file, which could be, say, changing from `/dev/zero` to a `shmem` file.

In this scenario, we no longer need the new file's reference because the existing VMA already maintains a reference to the equivalent underlying file! This raises questions about how references are managed when the merge happens, and I need to figure out how that is tracked.

I confirmed that if `is_mergeable_vma()` returns true, and `vma->vm_file == map->file`, the VMA and mapping can merge. The existing VMA already holds a reference to `map->file`.

Okay, so here's what's running through my head. If `vma_merge_new_range` somehow succeeds after `.mmap_prepare` swapped out the file, we have a problem. The existing VMA already has a reference to a `struct file` (`vma->vm_file`). If the merge happens, we're not dropping our reference to the "old" `map->file`. It would be a refcount leak. But, that's nearly impossible because `vma_merge_new_range` only succeeds if the files are the same, and if `.mmap_prepare` created a *new* file, the files won't be equal. For `/dev/zero` or anything where `file_doesnt_need_get = true` in `.mmap_prepare`, `vma_merge_new_range` *won't* happen. `__mmap_new_vma()` is always called, which means we avoid the refcount leak.

However, if a driver *did* return an existing file, and `is_mergeable_vma` returned true, then yes, we *would* leak a refcount because the merge happens. We need to remember that on success, `__mmap_complete` doesn't `fput(map->file)`. So, that's a subtle but potentially real problem we need to be careful of.

Okay, let's now focus on the failure path. The patch specifically addresses the error path of `__mmap_region`. If `map.file_doesnt_need_get` is true (meaning the driver provided a new file), and we have to abort, it calls `fput(map.file)`. That's good.

But here's a bigger issue: if `mmap_file` fails *after* `.mmap_prepare` swaps out the file, we have a double `fput()`! Inside `__mmap_new_vma`, if `mmap_file` fails, it calls `fput(vma->vm_file)`. We *know* `vma->vm_file == map->file`! This `fput` drops the extra reference provided by `.mmap_prepare`. But then, because `mmap_file` fails, `__mmap_new_vma` returns an error, triggering `abort_munmap` in `__mmap_region`. In `abort_munmap`, it checks `map.file_doesnt_need_get`, and *that's also true*. So it calls `fput(map.file)` again! We are double `fput()`-ing the same `struct file`, causing a potential use-after-free scenario. This is a critical bug. This is what the current patch is fixing.

To confirm this:
1.  `.mmap_prepare` swaps `map->file`, and sets `file_doesnt_need_get = true`.
2.  `__mmap_new_vma` creates a VMA, and assigns `vma->vm_file = map->file`.
3.  `mmap_file` fails.
4.  `fput(vma->vm_file)` is called. This drops the reference from `.mmap_prepare`.
5.  Error bubbles up to `abort_munmap`.
6.  `abort_munmap` sees `map.file_doesnt_need_get == true` and calls `fput(map->file)` *again*, leading to the double `fput`.

We must verify `mmap_file()` to ensure we are not missing anything else here. This is a critical code path.

    "thought": "**Analyzing a Potential Double `fput()` in `mmap` Implementation**

Alright, let's break this down. The core issue seems to revolve around the interaction between `vfs_mmap`, `__mmap_new_file_vma`, and the cleanup routines in case of errors, particularly the `abort_munmap` label. The original commit 605f6586ecf7 fixed a leak. Let's see if the fix introduced a bug.

It all starts with `mmap_file`, which ultimately calls `vfs_mmap`. If `vfs_mmap` fails, `mmap_file` returns the error. When `__mmap_new_file_vma` is called it calls `mmap_file`. If `mmap_file` fails, it calls `fput(vma->vm_file)`. `vma->vm_file` has ownership of the new file. This makes sense.

Now, if `mmap_file` fails *inside* `__mmap_new_file_vma`, and the driver hook changed the file (via `.mmap_prepare`), the `map.file_doesnt_need_get` flag is set to true. The critical thing here is: If `mmap_file` fails *in* `__mmap_new_file_vma`, then the code does `fput(vma->vm_file)`.

The trouble starts when we encounter an error and jump to `abort_munmap`. Inside `abort_munmap`, we check `map.file_doesnt_need_get`. This flag SHOULD indicate that the file refcount doesn't need to be decremented. However, the logic here is flawed. The new `fput` in `__mmap_new_file_vma()` is supposed to mean that the original refcount has been dropped and the kernel code shouldn't drop it again. But, since `map.file_doesnt_need_get` is true, the `abort_munmap` path will call `fput(map.file)`. This results in a *double* `fput()`!

Here's a breakdown of how the double free happens:

1.  `.mmap_prepare` changes the `vm_file` and sets `file_doesnt_need_get = true`
2.  `__mmap_new_file_vma` calls `mmap_file`.
3.  `mmap_file` fails, which means `mmap_file` returns an error,
4.  `__mmap_new_file_vma` calls `fput(vma->vm_file)`.
5.  `__mmap_new_vma` fails because of the error bubbling up
6.  The error bubbles up to `__mmap_region`.
7.  `__mmap_region` jumps to `abort_munmap`.
8.  `abort_munmap` calls `fput(map.file)` AGAIN because `map.file_doesnt_need_get` is still true (from step 1).

Aha! We have a race condition!

This double-free situation is the bug! So, how do we fix it? `__mmap_new_file_vma` *must* set `map->file_doesnt_need_get = false` *after* the `fput()` in the error path, if `.mmap_prepare` swapped the file. Alternatively, we could change `abort_munmap` so that it skips the `fput` if `vma->vm_file` is already NULL. But that's a less clean solution. The cleanest solution would be to change `__mmap_new_file_vma`.

Now, what about the "success" path? We *must* verify that the fix from the previous commit still works. If `vma_merge_new_range` succeeds, `map.file` would leak because we never call fput. Is merging possible? If `vma_merge_new_range` succeeds, is it a leak? Earlier I stated, "Suppose a driver's `.mmap_prepare` looks up an existing file and returns it, bumping its refcount." If it does this and it matches `vma->vm_file`, then `vma_merge_new_range` succeeds and we leak. So, how does the previous commit prevent the leak?

I must examine commit 605f6586ecf7 again to verify the solution.

    "content": null,
    "role": "assistant",
    "thought": "**Analysis of File Reference Counting and Potential Double `fput()` Vulnerability**

Okay, let me break this down. The core problem, as I see it, stems from how file references are handled in `ksys_mmap_pgoff()` when the `.mmap_prepare()` function of a device driver swaps the file associated with a memory map (VMA).

Here's my train of thought:

First, `ksys_mmap_pgoff()` gets a reference to the file with `fget()`, and `fput()` at the end. If the file is *not* swapped, a new reference is taken by the VMA with `get_file()`. Then the VMA releases this in its destructor, and the initial `fput()` in `ksys_mmap_pgoff()` drops the initial reference. But the situation is different if the file *is* swapped by `.mmap_prepare()`. In that case, the *new* file often already has a refcount of 1 (e.g., from `shmem_zero_setup_desc()`). Crucially, the VMA takes *ownership* of this existing reference. It does not need `get_file()`, and the original `ksys_mmap_pgoff()` drops the original reference.

Okay, that seems right, and was fixed with commit 605f6586ecf7.

Now, what about merging? If merging succeeds and we have a new file, `vma->vm_file` already holds a reference. But hold on, the swapped file's refcount is 1. If it's the *same* file as an adjacent VMA, `is_mergeable_vma` becomes true. In that case, we just return the address. That implies the driver's `.mmap_prepare` *returned an existing file* and bumped its reference count. If we merge, it doesn't get assigned to a *new* VMA, so the VMA doesn't get ownership of the new reference. The result is a leak since `ksys_mmap_pgoff()` only `fput()`s the *original* file. But is this possible? With a `/dev/zero`, a *new* file is allocated, so merging can't happen. The "merge leak" is only possible if a driver returns an existing file from `.mmap_prepare()`, and I don't know of any drivers that do that currently.

Now, for the error path and this patch. If `__mmap_new_vma` fails before `__mmap_new_file_vma` is called, all seems fine. For example, if `vma_start_write_killable()` or `vma_iter_prealloc()` fail, the code correctly calls `fput(map.file)` because `map.file_doesnt_need_get` is true.

But the issue is when `__mmap_new_vma()` fails *inside* `__mmap_new_file_vma()`. Specifically, if `mmap_file()` fails within `__mmap_new_file_vma()`, it first calls `fput(vma->vm_file)` to release the reference to the *swapped file*. Then `__mmap_new_vma()` returns an error, and `abort_munmap()` is called. The patch *then* calls `fput(map.file)`, but since `map.file_doesnt_need_get` is still true, the same `fput()` is called *again*!

The crucial point here is that `fput(vma->vm_file)` in `__mmap_new_file_vma()` *drops the only reference* to the swapped file. The swapped file has a refcount of 1. `fput(vma->vm_file)` reduces it to 0, and the file is freed. Then `abort_munmap` calls `fput(map.file)`. That's a classic use-after-free scenario!

Therefore, this *is* a real issue, and it looks like a double `fput()` vulnerability. We need to prevent the second `fput()` on the same file!

This is shared with both the\n * grow-up and grow-down cases.\n */\nstatic int acct_stack_growth(struct vm_area_struct *vma,\n\t\t\t     unsigned long size, unsigned long grow)\n{\n\tstruct mm_struct *mm = vma->vm_mm;\n\tunsigned long new_start;\n\n\t/* address space limit tests */\n\tif (!may_expand_vm(mm, &vma->flags, grow))\n\t\treturn -ENOMEM;\n\n\t/* Stack limit test */\n\tif (size > rlimit(RLIMIT_STACK))\n\t\treturn -ENOMEM;\n\n\t/* mlock limit tests */\n\tif (!mlock_future_ok(mm, vma_test(vma, VMA_LOCKED_BIT),\n\t\t\t     grow << PAGE_SHIFT))\n\t\treturn -ENOMEM;\n\n\t/* Check to ensure the stack will not grow into a hugetlb-only region */\n\tnew_start = vma->vm_end - size;\n#ifdef CONFIG_STACK_GROWSUP\n\tif (vma_test(vma, VMA_GROWSUP_BIT))\n\t\tnew_start = vma->vm_start;\n#endif\n\tif (is_hugepage_only_range(vma->vm_mm, new_start, size))\n\t\treturn -EFAULT;\n\n\t/*\n\t * Overcommit.. Consequently, `call_mmap_prepare` sets `map.file_doesnt_need_get = true` when `desc.vm_file != map.file`. This is the context for the double-free scenario.

The patch introduces an unconditional `fput(map.file)` on the error path if `map.file_doesnt_need_get` is true. However, `map.file_doesnt_need_get` remains true even if `__mmap_new_file_vma` has already called `fput()` on the file when `mmap_file` fails because `__mmap_new_file_vma` doesn't clear `map.file_doesnt_need_get`.

To verify, I check how `map` is handled: It's passed by pointer, meaning `__mmap_new_file_vma` can modify it. However, the code reveals `__mmap_new_file_vma` modifies `vma->vm_file` but not `map->file` nor `map->file_doesnt_need_get`. Hence, `map->file_doesnt_need_get` remains true after `__mmap_new_file_vma` returns, which, after going up to `__mmap_new_vma`, then up to `__mmap_region` calls `abort_munmap`, where the condition `if (map.file_doesnt_need_get)` is true. Consequently, the double `fput()` happens because `map->file` in `abort_munmap` is the same pointer as `vma->vm_file` before it was set to `NULL` in `__mmap_new_file_vma`.

To fix this, the patch author should have reset `map->file_doesnt_need_get = false` or `map->file = NULL` after `fput(vma->vm_file)` in the error path of `__mmap_new_file_vma`, to indicate the file has already been released. Or the condition `map->file_doesnt_need_get` can be checked again in `abort_munmap`.

Essentially, the patch introduces the double `fput()` bug by unconditionally calling `fput` in `abort_munmap` if `map.file_doesnt_need_get` is true.

Now, to investigate other potential issues. What if `mmap_file` succeeds, but `__mmap_new_vma` fails later? After a detailed review, it turns out that `__mmap_new_file_vma` is the only point of failure within `__mmap_new_vma` when `map->file` is present. Okay, let me break this down. It seems there's a serious problem in the `mmap` implementation, specifically when dealing with file mappings and potential errors during the `mmap_file()` call. The sequence is as follows:

First, `__mmap_new_file_vma()` is called, and a file gets mapped to a VMA. If `map->file_doesnt_need_get` is false, a `get_file()` is performed, incrementing the file's reference count. Then, `mmap_file()` attempts the actual mapping. Crucially, if that fails, `fput(vma->vm_file)` is called. And that call, if `map->file_doesnt_need_get` is true, effectively releases the only reference to the file.

Now, if `mmap_file()` *fails*, the code returns an error. This propagates up, eventually leading to `abort_munmap`. And `abort_munmap`, if the condition `map.file_doesnt_need_get` is true, calls `fput(map.file)`.

Here's the problem: if `map->file_doesnt_need_get` is true, meaning the *only* reference was the one created by VMA, we just called `fput` in `__mmap_new_file_vma`. Therefore, when `abort_munmap` is called, we're calling `fput` on a file that has already had its reference count dropped to zero. This, my friend, is a classic double-free vulnerability. Essentially, we're releasing the file twice! We have `fput` in two places!

This is a critical issue that needs immediate attention. I need to verify this logic carefully to confirm and then start thinking about potential mitigation strategies.

Okay, let's break this down. It appears we have a subtle, yet critical, double-free vulnerability within the kernel's memory mapping (`mmap`) code. The issue hinges around the interaction between `__mmap_new_file_vma()`, `mmap_prepare()`, and `abort_munmap()`, particularly when `file_doesnt_need_get` is true.

The core problem stems from how file references are managed when `mmap_prepare()` hands off a file to the mmap process. Essentially, we've identified a scenario where a file can be `fput` twice.

Here's my thought process:

1.  **Initial Setup:** The code path starts with `__mmap_new_file_vma()` called from `__mmap_new_vma()`. The critical conditional is dependent on the `map->file_doesnt_need_get` flag. This flag indicates whether `mmap_prepare` (through `call_mmap_prepare` and `vfs_mmap_prepare`) allocated and returned a new file, or had done `get_file` on an existing one. If the file doesn't need a `get`, then we skip `get_file()` in `__mmap_new_file_vma()` and if there is an error during `mmap_file`, `fput()` is called unconditionally on `vma->vm_file`.

2.  **The Double-Free Scenario:**
    *   If `mmap_file` subsequently returns an error, `fput(vma->vm_file)` *is* executed within `__mmap_new_file_vma()`. Critically, this `fput` is called even when `file_doesnt_need_get` is true.
    *   `__mmap_new_file_vma()` then returns an error, triggering a cleanup path, finally calling `abort_munmap()`.
    *   `abort_munmap()` *also* calls `fput(map.file)` *only* if `file_doesnt_need_get` is true.
    *   However, if `file_doesnt_need_get` is true, this indicates that the file was only referenced by `mmap_prepare` and `__mmap_new_file_vma()`'s initial assignment (`vma->vm_file = map->file;`) but not `get_file()`-d, meaning the first `fput` in `__mmap_new_file_vma()` will be the *only* reference.
    *   Therefore, the first `fput` *frees* the file and the second `fput` within `abort_munmap()` will cause a double-free.

3.  **Vulnerability Confirmation:** By carefully tracing the code path, especially considering the `file_doesnt_need_get` flag, it is apparent that if `mmap_file` fails, the first `fput` is the only release from the perspective of the mmap code. `abort_munmap` then triggers the second `fput` and double-free.

4.  **No mitigation**: The analysis found no other potential paths where the same file could be reference counted correctly (e.g. `vma_merge_new_range`) if `__mmap_new_file_vma` returns an error, confirming this double free.

**Conclusion:** The code has a clear use-after-free and double-free vulnerability if the `mmap_prepare` sets the file such that `file_doesnt_need_get` is set to `true`, and if `mmap_file` returns an error. This must be corrected to prevent a serious security flaw.

    "thought": "**Double-Free Vulnerability in Kernel mmap Path: `/dev/zero` and `shmem`**

Okay, so I'm looking at this `mmap` code, and I see a potential issue with the merging of VMAs, particularly when dealing with files like `/dev/zero`, which use shared memory.  My initial thought is that if a new mapping is created via `.mmap_prepare`, it shouldn't call `fput` under certain conditions.

The crux of the problem seems to be how `/dev/zero` (and by extension `shmem` in general) interacts with the VMA merging logic.  A file returned by `.mmap_prepare`, such as a new shmem file, gets a *different* `struct file *`. `is_mergeable_vma` relies on the VMAs having the *same* `file` pointer (`vma->vm_file == map->file`). Since `.mmap_prepare` *always* returns a new `shmem` file, it will *never* merge with an existing VMA. This is confirmed by how `/dev/zero` private mappings calls `shmem_zero_setup_desc()` which allocates a brand new `struct file *`.

This means `vma_merge_new_range` will consistently return `NULL` for these mappings, leading to `__mmap_new_vma` being called. Inside `__mmap_new_vma`, `__mmap_new_file_vma` sets `vma->vm_file = map->file` and calls `mmap_file()`. If `mmap_file()` fails, the code calls `fput(vma->vm_file)`, which is the same as `fput(map->file)`.  Then the function returns error, and execution jumps to `abort_munmap`.  Now, here's where it gets interesting: because `map.file_doesnt_need_get` is `true`, the code *again* calls `fput(map->file)`.  Boom, double free! This could lead to serious issues.

Now, I need to consider other paths. What if `mmap_file()` *succeeds*?  If `mmap_file` succeeds, then `__mmap_new_file_vma` returns 0.  Then `__mmap_new_vma` also returns 0, and `__mmap_region` proceeds to `__mmap_complete`. However, if `mmap_action_complete` returns an error, `__mmap_region` *directly* returns the error, skipping VMA unmapping. This seems a bit strange... Why isn't the VMA unmapped in this case? Is this another bug? No, the VMA is already in the tree. The system call returns an error, but the VMA remains mapped!

I should double-check the code for `mmap_action_complete` in `mm/vma.c` to confirm.

    "thought": "**Double Free/Memory Leak in mmap_prepare File Swap**

Okay, so I've been digging into the `mmap` code, specifically around how it handles file swaps within `mmap_prepare`.  My primary concern is the potential for double frees or leaks when a device driver's `mmap_prepare` function replaces the file backing a memory-mapped region.

The core issue boils down to how references to the swapped file are managed.  `mmap_prepare` can allocate a new file (let's call it `desc->vm_file`) and replace the original file with this new one (stored in `map->file`). The tricky part is ensuring that file references are correctly dropped in both success and failure scenarios.

Here's the problem: if `mmap_file` fails (after the file has been swapped), the code calls `fput(vma->vm_file)` *within* `__mmap_new_file_vma`, dropping the reference. However, the original code didn't reset a flag that indicates we no longer need to perform a cleanup in `abort_munmap`. So, `abort_munmap` then calls `fput(map.file)` again, leading to a double free of the file that was already freed in `__mmap_new_file_vma`. This happens because `map.file_doesnt_need_get` is still true, and `abort_munmap` incorrectly believes it owns a reference to the freed file.

There's a scenario that creates a subtle memory leak: the driver *succeeds* in returning the same file from `vfs_mmap_prepare` for a merge scenario. In this case, there's a +1 file reference that's never properly released if the newly created VMA merges into an existing one with the same file. I need to make sure I understand the code path that handles this, specifically where the reference from `mmap_prepare` is intended to be released (or if there is one).

This is a serious problem, and the fix is to clear `map->file_doesnt_need_get` in `__mmap_new_file_vma` after the `fput(vma->vm_file)` call when `mmap_file` fails. This ensures that `abort_munmap` doesn't attempt to `fput` a file that's already been freed.

I think the best fix is to set `map->file_doesnt_need_get = false` when `fput(vma->vm_file)` is called, so `abort_munmap` will know the cleanup has already been performed. This is not a double free if the fault injection fails in `vm_area_alloc()` in the first place, because then the flow skips this code path and falls through to the intended logic of calling `fput` in `abort_munmap`.

Okay, so I've been digging into this mmap code, and I've found a couple of issues related to file reference counting and memory management, specifically where the mmap prepare function and the merge functionality interact. Let me break down what I've discovered:

First, the original code had these checks:

1.  `__mmap_new_file_vma()` - skips `get_file()`.
2.  `abort_munmap:` in `__mmap_region()` - does `fput()`.

I realized there's a problem when `__mmap_region()` successfully merges VMAs. When that happens, and `allocated_new = false`.

If the driver returns the SAME file for multiple mappings,  `file_doesnt_need_get` is `true`. I then noted these critical points:

*   The `mmap_prepare` function can return the same file as an existing mapping.
*   If `vma_merge_new_range` succeeds, `__mmap_new_vma` is skipped.
*   The issue is, even if we merge successfully (and the driver returns a shared file), `get_file()` is skipped, leaving the additional reference from `mmap_prepare` *un*managed and, thus, LEAKED.

Okay, let's look at the scenarios:

1.  **Double-Free on `mmap_file()` Error:** Here's the core issue I've identified. If `mmap_file()` returns an error, `__mmap_new_file_vma()` executes:
    *   `mmap_file()` fails.
    *   The code then calls `fput(vma->vm_file)`, dropping the reference from the driver that was supposed to be transferred.
    *   `__mmap_region()` catches the error.
    *   `abort_munmap` then tries to `fput(map.file)`.
    *   However, `map.file` and `vma->vm_file` point to the same file, which has already been freed in the previous step.

    This creates a clear double-free vulnerability, which is introduced by the patch.

    To fix this double-free, `__mmap_new_file_vma()` should set `map->file_doesnt_need_get = false` after freeing.

2.  **Memory Leak on Merge Success:** This is the second issue. If `vma_merge_new_range()` succeeds, and `desc->vm_file != map->file`, `map->file_doesnt_need_get` is set to `true`.

    *   Now, a crucial point: If the driver's mmap\_prepare returns a SINGLE shared file for *all* mappings, a merge can succeed.
    *   If merge succeeds, then `__mmap_new_vma` is skipped. The new reference acquired by driver `.mmap_prepare` is leaked, because we don't transfer it to the VMA, and we do not `fput` it.

    This leak already existed before this patch, but the patch attempts to fix leaks on error conditions, and this leak on successful merge is *still* present.

**Double Free Analysis (Deeper Dive):**

Okay, let's verify how the double free happens in the code. I'll trace how the pointer is freed twice.

1.  `call_mmap_prepare` sets `map->file_doesnt_need_get = true` and `map->file = new_file`.
2.  `__mmap_new_vma` allocates `vma`.
3.  `__mmap_new_file_vma` assigns `vma->vm_file = map->file`. It skips `get_file()` because `map->file_doesnt_need_get` is true.
4.  `mmap_file(vma->vm_file, vma)` is called and returns an error.
5.  `__mmap_new_file_vma` error handling calls `fput(vma->vm_file)`. This drops the 1 reference on `new_file`. `new_file` is freed.
6.  `vma->vm_file = NULL`.
7.  `__mmap_new_file_vma` returns error.
8.  `__mmap_new_vma` frees `vma` and returns error.
9.  `__mmap_region` jumps to `abort_munmap`.
10.

However, the `__mmap_region` function has a critical section (`abort_munmap`) that also attempts to `fput(map.file)` under certain error conditions. The key is `map.file_doesnt_need_get`. This flag indicates that the file reference was *not* acquired with a `get_file()`, implying a different ownership model. Specifically when `map->file` is from `mmap_prepare`, such as when using `/dev/zero`, the file is created, its refcount is at 1, and `map.file_doesnt_need_get` is set to `true`.     "thought": "**My Immediate Concerns Regarding `mmap_action_complete()` Failure**

Okay, so I'm looking at this `__mmap_region` code again, specifically how it handles errors after `mmap_action_complete()`.  My immediate concern is this: if `mmap_action_complete()` fails, it looks like it just returns the error code directly. The problem is, it leaves a newly allocated VMA attached to the mapping file, and that the file may have been replaced, which could lead to resource leaks and potential corruption.

Wait, it just returns `error` immediately from `__mmap_region`. The mapped VMA IS NOT unmapped! This is a potential disaster! The function should handle this condition or it could lead to vulnerabilities.

This means we have an allocated VMA that's now linked, potentially, to an inconsistent mapping, right? That’s a serious issue! I need to dig into `mmap_action_finish` and understand what it handles. I need to understand if the cleanup is handled there, or if we have a gaping hole in memory management after a failure here. This needs more investigation, and fast.

    "content": "{"content":"\nstatic int mmap_action_finish(struct vm_area_struct *vma,\n\t\t\t      struct mmap_action *action, int err)\n{\n\tsize_t len;\n\n\tif (!err)\n\t\terr = call_vma_mapped(vma);\n\tif (!err && action->success_hook)\n\t\terr = action->success_hook(vma);\n\n\t/* do_munmap() might take rmap lock, so release if held. */\n\tmaybe_rmap_unlock_action(vma, action);\n\tif (!err)\n\t\treturn 0;\n\n\t/*\n\t * If an error occurs, unmap the VMA altogether and return an error. We\n\t * only clear the newly allocated VMA, since this function is only\n\t * invoked if we do NOT merge, so we only clean up the VMA we created.\n\t */\n\tlen = vma_pages(vma) << PAGE_SHIFT;\n\tdo_munmap(current->mm, vma->vm_start, len, NULL);\n\tif (action->error_hook) {\n\t\t/* We may want to filter the error. */\n\t\terr = action->error_hook(err);\n\t\t/* The caller should not clear the error. */\n\t\tVM_WARN_ON_ONCE(!err);\n\t}\n\treturn err;\n}\n\n#ifdef CONFIG_MMU\n/**\n * mmap_action_prepare -     "thought": "**Double-Put Vulnerability in Kernel mmap Implementation**

Okay, here's what's happening. I've been tracing the memory mapping logic, specifically around the `__mmap_region` function, and a recent patch meant to fix a memory leak. I've found a critical double-free or Use-After-Free bug introduced by that patch. It's related to the interaction between `.mmap_prepare` (a file operation hook), and how the kernel handles file references during memory mapping failures.

Here's the problem: The original code, as far as I can see, was fine and did *not* leak file references. `mmap_action_finish()` correctly calls `do_munmap()` in case of an error, which unmaps the VMA and calls `fput()` and handles the error gracefully. But the bug is in a specific error path where a `.mmap_prepare` function can swap the underlying file for a VMA. If there's an error later in the process *after* `.mmap_prepare` has already swapped in a new file, but before the VMA is fully finalized, we end up with this double free.

Specifically, the `__mmap_new_file_vma` function is responsible for linking a file to a new VMA. It conditionally gets a file reference using `get_file()` but always puts that reference in `fput()` if `.mmap_file()` fails. If `.mmap_prepare` replaces the original file and sets `file_doesnt_need_get` to true, `get_file()` is skipped and a subsequent `fput()` from `__mmap_new_file_vma` drops the *only* reference, freeing the file. Then, in the `abort_munmap` section, the original file is also passed to an fput call. Because of the previous call, the file has been already freed! This leads to the Use-After-Free!

The commit attempted to fix a memory leak caused by `vm_area_alloc()` failing *before* a new file was swapped in, because this resulted in the original file leaking. The author added an `fput()` in `abort_munmap` to handle this specific *leak* condition. However, it *didn't* consider that an error *inside* `__mmap_new_file_vma` (like a failure of `mmap_file()`) would result in *already* freeing the file. This makes the `fput()` at `abort_munmap` a double-free.

I've carefully verified that `__mmap_new_file_vma` unconditionally `fput()`'s on errors, meaning the new patch has made the problem worse. The author missed the fact that `__mmap_new_file_vma` ALREADY drops the reference to the *new* file when there is an error.

The bug lies in this sequence: 1) `.mmap_prepare` swaps a file. 2) `__mmap_new_file_vma` gets the new file. 3) `mmap_file()` fails. `__mmap_new_file_vma` correctly `fput()`s the file, freeing it. 4) The `abort_munmap` path runs and incorrectly calls `fput()` on the already freed object.

The correct fix needs to clear `file_doesnt_need_get` or set `map.file = NULL` *before* the reference is consumed, to avoid this double-free. Or it needs to be carefully handled to avoid the use-after-free. I think this represents a significant security vulnerability, and I'm flagging this for immediate review.

Okay, I've got a handle on this patch and the bug it introduces. Here's what I've found, working through it step-by-step.

Firstly, the patch's main goal is to fix a leak when `__mmap_new_vma` fails, particularly when a virtual memory area allocation (`vm_area_alloc`) fails. The intention is to free the associated file with `fput(map.file)` if `map.file_doesnt_need_get` is true. However, it completely backfires.

Here's the sequence: If `__mmap_new_vma` proceeds to `__mmap_new_file_vma` and `mmap_file()` fails (because `f_op->mmap` returns an error), `__mmap_new_file_vma` cleans up by calling `fput(vma->vm_file)` which is the same file and releases the single reference, as `get_file()` was skipped. The patch code unconditionally calls `fput(map.file)` again because `map.file_doesnt_need_get` is still true, and `map.file` wasn't cleared. This is where the double-put/Use-After-Free occurs because `map.file` is now freed. This is bad; the patch *introduces* a UAF!

I initially wondered about other error paths. What happens if `mmap_action_complete` fails? But if `mmap_action_complete` fails, the error goes directly, bypassing `abort_munmap`. And `mmap_action_complete` itself calls the appropriate `fput` in `mmap_action_finish`. So that bypasses the double-free. Also, since we're bypassing `abort_munmap`, the charged memory is not a concern, as it is only relevant for anonymous memory which does not use the file-path. That's a red herring. And what about `vms_abort_munmap_vmas`? I remembered that `vms_complete_munmap_vmas` handles cleanup already, so bypassing `abort_munmap` in that case is *correct*. And if `__mmap_new_vma` fails, then `vms_abort_munmap_vmas` reattaches everything so that's good.

So, the central issue boils down to the double `fput()` on the `mmap_file()` error path inside `__mmap_new_file_vma()`. I should summarize this clearly:

**Type:** Double-free / Use-After-Free.
**Description:** The patch introduces a double-free on the error path when `mmap_file()` fails inside `__mmap_new_file_vma()`.

    "content": "squad_1_thought
Ah! I see.
```c
	err = vfs_mmap_prepare(map->file, desc);
	if (err)
		return err;

	err = call_action_prepare(map, desc);
	if (err)
		return err;

	/* Update fields permitted to be changed. */
	map->pgoff = desc->pgoff;
	if (desc->vm_file != map->file) {
		map->file_doesnt_need_get = true;
		map->file = desc->vm_file;
	}
```
If `vfs_mmap_prepare` fails, `map->file_doesnt_need_get` is false.
If `call_action_prepare` fails, `map->file_doesnt_need_get` is false.
But wait, what if `vfs_mmap_prepare` (which calls `f_op->mmap_prepare`) replaces `desc->vm_file`, and *then* returns an error?
If the driver's `mmap_prepare` replaces `desc->vm_file` and then returns an error, the driver is supposed to `fput()` the new file it allocated before returning an error. That is standard behavior: if you fail, you clean up your own mess.

But what if `vfs_mmap_prepare` SUCCEEDS, replacing `desc->vm_file`, and *then* `call_action_prepare(map, desc)` FAILS?
If `call_action_prepare` fails, `call_mmap_prepare` returns `err` WITHOUT executing the block:
```c
	if (desc->vm_file != map->file) {
		map->file_doesnt_need_get = true;
		map->file = desc->vm_file;
	}
```
Because it returns early!
```c
	err = call_action_prepare(map, desc);
	if (err)
		return err;
```
If `call_action_prepare` fails, `map->file_doesnt_need_get` remains `false`.
Then `__mmap_region` receives the error, goes to `abort_munmap`:
```c
abort_munmap:
	if (map.file_doesnt_need_get)
		fput(map.file);
	vms_abort_munmap_vmas(&map.vms, &map.mas_detach);
	return error;
```
It sees `map.file_doesnt_need_get` is `false`, so it DOES NOT `fput(map.file)`. BUT `desc->vm_file` was allocated and replaced by `vfs_mmap_prepare`, and now we leaked it!
Because `map->file` wasn't updated to `desc->vm_file`, `map->file` is still the original file, and the original file will be `fput`ted by `ksys_mmap_pgoff`. The NEW file in `desc->vm_file` is leaked!

Wait, let's verify if `call_action_prepare(map, desc)` can actually fail.
What is `call_action_prepare`?",

    "content": "squad_1_thought
Aha! So `call_action_prepare(map, desc)` calls `mmap_action_prepare(desc)`.
And `mmap_action_prepare()` can definitely return errors.
Wait, does `mmap_prepare()` itself set `desc->action` to something else that then causes `mmap_action_prepare` to fail?
Yes! `f_op->mmap_prepare(desc)` can set `desc->action.type = MMAP_REMAP_PFN`, and then `mmap_action_prepare(desc)` might return an error (e.g. out of memory, invalid arguments, etc).
If `mmap_action_prepare(desc)` fails, `call_action_prepare()` returns an error.
Then `call_mmap_prepare` returns the error BEFORE it sets `map->file = desc->vm_file` and `map->file_doesnt_need_get = true;`!

Let's look at `call_mmap_prepare`:
```c
static int call_mmap_prepare(struct mmap_state *map,
		struct vm_area_desc *desc)
{
	int err;

	/* Invoke the hook. */
	err = vfs_mmap_prepare(map->file, desc);
	if (err)
		return err;

	err = call_action_prepare(map, desc);
	if (err)
		return err;

	/* Update fields permitted to be changed. */
	map->pgoff = desc->pgoff;
	if (desc->vm_file != map->file) {
		map->file_doesnt_need_get = true;
		map->file = desc->vm_file;
	}
...
}
```

If `vfs_mmap_prepare` succeeds, it might have replaced `desc->vm_file`.
If `call_action_prepare(map, desc)` then fails, `call_mmap_prepare` returns `err`.
Back in `__mmap_region`:
```c
	if (!error && have_mmap_prepare)
		error = call_mmap_prepare(&map, &desc);
	if (error)
		goto abort_munmap;

...

abort_munmap:
	if (map.file_doesnt_need_get)
		fput(map.file);
	vms_abort_munmap_vmas(&map.vms, &map.mas_detach);
	return error;
```
If `call_mmap_prepare` returns `err` after `vfs_mmap_prepare` replaced `desc->vm_file`, then `map.file_doesnt_need_get` is STILL `false` (because `call_mmap_prepare` returned early). And `map.file` is STILL the original file!
Thus:
1. `desc->vm_file` holds the new file, which is leaked because nothing cleans it up.
2. `abort_munmap` does NOT `fput(map.file)` (the original file) because `map.file_doesnt_need_get` is false.
Wait, if `map.file_doesnt_need_get` is false, it means `ksys_mmap_pgoff()` will `fput()` the ORIGINAL file. That's correct for the original file. BUT the NEW file is leaked!

Is there any other path where `desc->vm_file` is changed, but `map.file` is not updated?
What if `__mmap_new_vma()` fails?
```c
	/* ...but if we can't, allocate a new VMA. */
	if (!vma) {
		error = __mmap_new_vma(&map, &vma, &desc.action);
		if (error)
			goto unacct_error;
		allocated_new = true;
	}
```
If `__mmap_new_vma()` fails, we goto `unacct_error` which goes to `abort_munmap`.
At this point, `call_mmap_prepare` has already succeeded!
So `map.file_doesnt_need_get` is `true`, and `map.file` is the NEW file.
```c
abort_munmap:
	/*
	 * This indicates that .mmap_prepare has set a new file, differing from
	 * desc->vm_file. But since we're aborting the operation, only the
	 * original file will be cleaned up. Ensure we clean up both.
	 */
	if (map.file_doesnt_need_get)
		fput(map.file);
```
So the new code `fput(map.file)` will correctly free the NEW file if `__mmap_new_vma()` fails.
Wait, if `map.file_doesnt_need_get` is true, does it mean the new file needs to be `fput`ted?
Let's check `ksys_mmap_pgoff()` to see what it `fput`s.",

```c
unsigned long ksys_mmap_pgoff(unsigned long addr, unsigned long len,
			      unsigned long prot, unsigned long flags,
			      unsigned long fd, unsigned long pgoff)
{
...
	if (!(flags & MAP_ANONYMOUS)) {
		audit_mmap_fd(fd, flags);
		file = fget(fd);
		if (!file)
			return -EBADF;
...
	retval = vm_mmap_pgoff(file, addr, len, prot, flags, pgoff);
out_fput:
	if (file)
		fput(file);
	return retval;
}
```
Yes, `ksys_mmap_pgoff()` ALWAYS `fput`s the original file after `vm_mmap_pgoff()` returns, REGARDLESS of whether `vm_mmap_pgoff` succeeds or fails!
Wait, but if `vm_mmap_pgoff` succeeds, how does the VMA hold a reference to the file?
Ah! `__mmap_new_file_vma` does this:
```c
static int __mmap_new_file_vma(struct mmap_state *map,
			       struct vm_area_struct *vma)
{
...
	vma->vm_file = map->file;
	if (!map->file_doesnt_need_get)
		get_file(map->file);
```
So if `file_doesnt_need_get` is false (meaning the original file was kept), `__mmap_new_file_vma` calls `get_file()` to acquire an EXTRA reference for the VMA. Then `ksys_mmap_pgoff()` drops its own reference.
If `file_doesnt_need_get` is true (meaning the driver swapped the file and the replacement file ALREADY has a reference count of 1 for the VMA), `__mmap_new_file_vma` DOES NOT call `get_file()`. The VMA takes ownership of the single reference of the NEW file. `ksys_mmap_pgoff()` still drops its reference to the ORIGINAL file.

So:
If `mmap_region()` SUCCEEDS with a swapped file:
- `vma->vm_file` = NEW file.
- NEW file refcount is 1 (from when it was allocated in `mmap_prepare`). No `get_file()` called.
- ORIGINAL file refcount was N. `ksys_mmap_pgoff()` calls `fput(ORIGINAL file)`, dropping to N-1.

If `mmap_region()` FAILS after `call_mmap_prepare()` successfully swaps the file:
- NEW file has refcount 1.
- `abort_munmap` is reached.
- The patched code says:
  ```c
  if (map.file_doesnt_need_get)
      fput(map.file);
  ```
  Since `map.file_doesnt_need_get` is true and `map.file` is the NEW file, this `fput(map.file)` correctly drops the reference on the NEW file to 0, freeing it!
- `ksys_mmap_pgoff()` will still call `fput(ORIGINAL file)` because its local `file` variable points to the original file.

Wait, what if `call_mmap_prepare()` FAILS *after* swapping the file?
Let's trace `call_mmap_prepare` again:

Then `__mmap_region` sees the error, goes to `abort_munmap`:

Aha! `remap_pfn_range_prepare` can return `-EFAULT` or `-EINVAL` (from `get_remap_pgoff`).
So `call_action_prepare()` CAN fail!
If `call_action_prepare()` fails, `call_mmap_prepare()` returns `-EFAULT` or `-EINVAL`.

Let's trace `__mmap_region()` again when this happens:
```c
	error = __mmap_setup(&map, &desc, uf);
	if (!error && have_mmap_prepare)
		error = call_mmap_prepare(&map, &desc);
	if (error)
		goto abort_munmap;
```

Inside `call_mmap_prepare`:
```c
static int call_mmap_prepare(struct mmap_state *map,
		struct vm_area_desc *desc)
{
	int err;

	/* Invoke the hook. */
	err = vfs_mmap_prepare(map->file, desc);
	if (err)
		return err;

	err = call_action_prepare(map, desc);
	if (err)
		return err;

	/* Update fields permitted to be changed. */
	map->pgoff = desc->pgoff;
	if (desc->vm_file != map->file) {
		map->file_doesnt_need_get = true;
		map->file = desc->vm_file;
	}
...
}
```
If `vfs_mmap_prepare` (which calls the driver's `mmap_prepare` callback) swaps the file, `desc->vm_file` becomes the NEW file. The original file is still in `map->file`.
Then `call_action_prepare` is called.
Suppose the driver set `desc->action.type = MMAP_REMAP_PFN` with invalid bounds, causing `mmap_action_prepare` to return `-EFAULT`.
Then `call_action_prepare` returns `-EFAULT`.
`call_mmap_prepare` returns `-EFAULT`.
Notice that `map->file` IS STILL the ORIGINAL file, and `map->file_doesnt_need_get` IS STILL false!
And `desc->vm_file` IS THE NEW FILE.
But `desc` is just a local variable on the stack of `__mmap_region`. The pointer to the new file is lost.

Back in `__mmap_region`:
```c
abort_munmap:
	/*
	 * This indicates that .mmap_prepare has set a new file, differing from
	 * desc->vm_file. But since we're aborting the operation, only the
	 * original file will be cleaned up. Ensure we clean up both.
	 */
	if (map.file_doesnt_need_get)
		fput(map.file);
	vms_abort_munmap_vmas(&map.vms, &map.mas_detach);
	return error;
```
Here, `map.file_doesnt_need_get` is false.
So `fput(map.file)` is skipped. (And `map.file` is the original file, which is fine to skip here since `ksys_mmap_pgoff` will `fput` it later).
But the NEW file (`desc.vm_file`) has a refcount of 1 and is never `fput`ted. It is permanently leaked!

Wait, is there any cleanup of `desc.vm_file`?
Let's see if `desc.vm_file` is cleaned up anywhere in `__mmap_region()`.
`desc` is defined as:
```c
	struct vm_area_desc desc = {
		.mm = mm,
		.file = file,
		.action = {
			.type = MMAP_NOTHING, /* Default to no further action. */
		},
	};
```
And it's not touched after `goto abort_munmap` because `__mmap_region` just returns `error`.
So YES, if `call_action_prepare` fails, the new file is leaked!

Wait! Does `__mmap_region` check if `desc.vm_file` != `map.file` in the error path?
No, it does this:
```c
abort_munmap:
	if (map.file_doesnt_need_get)
		fput(map.file);
```

`differing from desc->vm_file.`

The comment is weird: "This indicates that .mmap_prepare has set a new file, differing from desc->vm_file."
Wait, if `.mmap_prepare` replaces the file, it modifies `desc->vm_file`. The ORIGINAL file was in `map.file`.
Then `call_mmap_prepare` updates `map.file = desc.vm_file` and sets `map.file_doesnt_need_get = true`.
So if `map.file_doesnt_need_get` is true, `map.file` has the NEW file. The comment probably meant "differing from the original file".

But as I found, if `call_action_prepare` fails, `map.file` is NEVER updated!
And `desc.vm_file` holds the new file.
And `map.file_doesnt_need_get` is false.
So the leaked file is in `desc.vm_file`.
Since `desc.vm_file != file` (the original file passed to `__mmap_region`), the new file is leaked!
This is a clear memory leak on the error path where `call_action_prepare` fails.

Yes! If `call_action_prepare(map, desc)` fails, `call_mmap_prepare` returns `err` WITHOUT updating `map->file_doesnt_need_get` and `map->file`.
The new file is lost, as `desc` goes out of scope in `__mmap_region`.
This is a memory leak vector.

Is there any other security concern?
Let's see the patch again.
```c
 abort_munmap:
+	/*
+	 * This indicates that .mmap_prepare has set a new file, differing from
+	 * desc->vm_file. But since we're aborting the operation, only the
+	 * original file will be cleaned up. Ensure we clean up both.
+	 */
+	if (map.file_doesnt_need_get)
+		fput(map.file);
```
Wait! Is it safe to call `fput(map.file)` here?
What if `ksys_mmap_pgoff()` is NOT the caller, and another caller doesn't expect `map.file` to be freed if `__mmap_region` fails?
Wait, if `__mmap_region` fails, no VMA is created.
The ORIGINAL file is expected to be cleaned up by the CALLER (e.g., `ksys_mmap_pgoff`, which calls `fput(file)`).
If `.mmap_prepare` swapped the file, the NEW file has 1 reference (from its creation/get inside `.mmap_prepare`), and the caller knows nothing about the NEW file. The caller ONLY knows about the ORIGINAL file.
So the caller will `fput()` the ORIGINAL file.
The NEW file must be `fput()` by `__mmap_region()`, which is what this patch tries to do.
So `fput(map.file)` is correct for the NEW file.

But wait! What if `map.file_doesnt_need_get` is true, but `map.file` was somehow passed in with that flag already set?
Where is `map` initialized?
In `__mmap_region()`:
```c
	MMAP_STATE(map, mm, &vmi, addr, len, pgoff, vma_flags, file);
```
`MMAP_STATE` initializes `map`. Since `file_doesnt_need_get` is not explicitly set, it is 0.
Then `map.file_doesnt_need_get` becomes true ONLY if `.mmap_prepare` replaces the file.

Ah, `vfs_mmap_prepare` just calls `file->f_op->mmap_prepare(desc)`.
If `mmap_prepare` fails, does it leak the file?
If `f_op->mmap_prepare` replaces `desc->vm_file` and THEN returns an error, it's a bug in the driver. A well-behaved function shouldn't return an error while leaving modified output parameters that need cleanup.

BUT `call_action_prepare(map, desc)` is called AFTER `mmap_prepare(desc)` succeeds.
If `mmap_prepare` succeeded, `desc->vm_file` now contains the new file.
Then `call_action_prepare` is called.
If `call_action_prepare` fails, `call_mmap_prepare` returns its error.
The driver (which already returned success) doesn't know about this failure, so it cannot clean up the new file!
And `call_mmap_prepare` doesn't clean it up either, it just returns the error.
And because `call_mmap_prepare` returns early, `map.file` is NOT updated to `desc.vm_file`, nor is `map.file_doesnt_need_get` set to true.
So `abort_munmap` in `__mmap_region()` will only clean up `map.file` if `map.file_doesnt_need_get` is true (which it isn't), leaving `desc.vm_file` leaked!

Wait, is there any OTHER problem with the patch itself?
Let's look at the patch in `abort_munmap`:
```c
abort_munmap:
