```c
	 * This indicates that .mmap_prepare has set a new file, differing from
	 * desc->vm_file. But since we're aborting the operation, only the
	 * original file will be cleaned up. Ensure we clean up both.
	 */
	if (map.file_doesnt_need_get)
		fput(map.file);
	vms_abort_munmap_vmas(&map.vms, &map.mas_detach);
	return error;
```
If `map.file_doesnt_need_get` is true, `map.file` is the swapped file, and we `fput(map.file)`.
What about the ORIGINAL file?
Wait, if `map.file_doesnt_need_get` is true, it means `map.file` was REPLACED.
The ORIGINAL file was in `map.file` BEFORE the swap.
But after the swap, `map.file = desc.vm_file`.
Wait, the original file is NO LONGER inside `map` or `desc`!
Is the original file still available?
Yes, the original file is still in the local variable `file` in `__mmap_region(struct file *file, ...)` and is returned to the caller of `__mmap_region` (e.g., `mmap_region`).
Then `mmap_region` returns the error to `do_mmap` -> `vm_mmap_pgoff` -> `ksys_mmap_pgoff`.
`ksys_mmap_pgoff` has its own local variable `file` which is the original file, and it calls `fput(file)` on the out path!
So the original file is correctly `fput`ted.
And the NEW file is `fput`ted by `__mmap_region` error path because `map.file` now holds the new file.
Wait! If `map.file` holds the NEW file, and `__mmap_region` calls `fput(map.file)`, is `map.file` still referenced somewhere else?
No, it has exactly 1 reference from when it was created.
BUT wait! What if the caller of `__mmap_region` also tries to `fput` the new file? No, the caller only knows the old file.

Is there any double `fput()`?
What if `mmap_prepare` replaces the file with a new file, but does NOT allocate a new file, but instead returns another file that ALREADY has a reference, and does NOT take a new reference? No, it has to return a reference. If it doesn't need get, the reference is transferred to the VMA.

Ah! If `mmap_action_complete` fails, it calls `do_munmap()` on the newly created VMA!
```c
	do_munmap(current->mm, vma->vm_start, len, NULL);
```
Since the VMA was already fully inserted into the maple tree by `__mmap_complete(&map, vma)`, it unmaps the VMA.
When it unmaps the VMA, `do_munmap` (or `do_vmi_munmap`) will naturally call `fput(vma->vm_file)` as part of removing the VMA!
So the NEW file IS properly freed if `mmap_action_complete` fails.

Wait, what if `__mmap_region` returns an error directly when `mmap_action_complete` fails:
```c
	if (have_mmap_prepare && allocated_new) {
		error = mmap_action_complete(vma, &desc.action);
		if (error)
			return error;
	}
```
In this path, `__mmap_region` does NOT go to `abort_munmap`. It just returns `error`.
And remember `ksys_mmap_pgoff()` ALWAYS calls `fput(file)` on the ORIGINAL file on error!
Wait. If `ksys_mmap_pgoff` calls `fput(original_file)` AND `do_munmap` unmaps the VMA (which holds the NEW file, so it `fput`s the NEW file), BOTH files are correctly freed!
But wait... If `map.file_doesnt_need_get` is true, the NEW file's reference was TRANSFERRED to the VMA.
When `do_munmap` removes the VMA, it will call `fput` on the NEW file. That drops its refcount to 0 and frees it.
And `ksys_mmap_pgoff()` still holds the ORIGINAL file and will call `fput` on it.
This seems correct.

But wait, what about the case where `__mmap_new_vma` DOES merge with an adjacent VMA?
```c
	/* Attempt to merge with adjacent VMAs... */
	if (map.prev || map.next) {
		VMG_MMAP_STATE(vmg, &map, /* vma = */ NULL);

		vma = vma_merge_new_range(&vmg);
	}
```
If it merges successfully, `allocated_new` is false.

Wait! `vma_merge_new_range` merges the new range into an existing VMA.
If it merges, does it do anything with `map.file`?
No, it just uses the file from the existing VMA! The existing VMA ALREADY holds a reference to that file.
Wait, if it merges, does it mean the new range was backed by the SAME file (as determined by `is_mergeable_vma` and `file == vma->vm_file`)?
Yes! `is_mergeable_vma` checks `file == vma->vm_file`.
So if `map.file` was swapped by `.mmap_prepare` to `desc.vm_file`, then `map.file` is the NEW file.
Then `vma_merge_new_range` checks if `map.file == vma->vm_file` of the adjacent VMA.
If they are equal, it merges.
But wait! If they are equal, it means the driver returned a file that ALREADY BACKS an adjacent VMA!
In that case, the adjacent VMA already has a reference to the file.
But wait! The `.mmap_prepare` ALSO gave us a reference to this file! Because we set `map.file_doesnt_need_get = true`!
This means the new mapping HAS a reference to the file.
If we MERGE, `vma_merge_new_range` just expands the existing VMA, and DOES NOT KEEP the new reference!
Wait, if it doesn't keep the new reference, what happens to the reference we got from `.mmap_prepare`?
Let's see: `__mmap_region()` returns `addr`.
If `allocated_new == false`, it bypasses `mmap_action_complete`.
And it just returns `addr`!
It NEVER calls `fput(map.file)` on success when it merges!
This means if a file swapped by `.mmap_prepare` successfully merges with an adjacent VMA, the reference acquired by `.mmap_prepare` is LEAKED!

Then `__mmap_region` calls `vma_merge_new_range`.
If `vma_merge_new_range` succeeds, it returns the expanded `vma`.
Then `__mmap_region` sets `allocated_new = false`.
Then `__mmap_complete(&map, vma)` is called. (Does it `fput`?)
Let's check `__mmap_complete`.",

Wait! `vma_merge_new_range` expands an adjacent VMA. It doesn't modify refcounts of files! The file reference acquired by `mmap_prepare()` is indeed leaked if it's merged.
Let's check `shmem_zero_setup` and `mmap_zero_prepare` to see if they can trigger a merge.
`shmem_zero_setup_desc()` allocates a new file.
If it allocates a new file, it cannot merge with an existing VMA, because the existing VMA would have a DIFFERENT file pointer (even if it's also a shmem_zero file, `alloc_file_pseudo` gives a distinct `struct file *`).
So `vma_merge_new_range` will fail because `file != vma->vm_file`.
Wait, what if the driver's `mmap_prepare` returns a SINGLETON file? E.g., a globally shared `struct file` that was already used in a previous mapping?
If it's a singleton file, then the previous mapping HAS that file pointer.
If the driver returns that same file pointer, `file == vma->vm_file`!
But if it's a singleton, the driver presumably called `get_file()` before setting it in `desc->vm_file`.
If `map.file_doesnt_need_get` is true, it means `mmap_prepare` gave us a reference that we are responsible for.
If we MERGE, `__mmap_region` drops this reference! Oh no wait, it DOESN'T drop it! It LEAKS it!

Let's trace `ksys_mmap_pgoff()` if we merge.
If we merge, `__mmap_region()` returns `addr`.
`ksys_mmap_pgoff()` then calls `fput(file)` on the ORIGINAL file.
But the NEW file (the singleton with a bumped refcount) NEVER gets its refcount decremented because we merged and `vma_merge_new_range` doesn't consume the reference and `__mmap_region` doesn't `fput` it either!

Aha! `__shmem_zero_setup()` allocates a NEW file.
If it allocates a new file, it means `file != vma->vm_file` for ANY adjacent VMA.
Why? Because `alloc_file_pseudo()` creates a distinct `struct file` every time it's called.
So if we mmap `/dev/zero` multiple times, EACH mapping gets its OWN new `struct file` from `shmem_zero_setup()`.
Since `vma->vm_file` will be unique, `is_mergeable_vma()` will NEVER return true for merging two `/dev/zero` shared mappings.
Wait, let's verify `is_mergeable_vma()`.",

    "content": "squad_1_thought
Yes, `is_mergeable_vma` checks `vma->vm_file != vmg->file`.
And earlier I found that `vma_merge_new_range` expands an adjacent VMA.
If `vmg->file` is a singleton file (say, `file_X`), and `vma->vm_file` of the adjacent VMA is ALSO `file_X`, they will merge.
When they merge, the existing VMA's `vm_file` remains `file_X`.
But the new mapping attempt acquired an ADDITIONAL reference to `file_X` (because `map.file_doesnt_need_get = true` transferred ownership of a reference from `.mmap_prepare` to the new mapping attempt).
Does `vma_merge_new_range` drop this additional reference?
Let's see: `vma_merge_new_range` just modifies the adjacent VMA's length. It doesn't touch any file refcount.
So the additional reference is returned to `__mmap_region`.
`__mmap_region` sets `allocated_new = false`, and eventually returns `addr` (success).
It NEVER drops the extra reference to `file_X` !
This means every time we successfully merge a mapping backed by a file that `.mmap_prepare` swapped (and gave us a reference to), we LEAK that file reference!

Let me double check this logic.
If `call_mmap_prepare` swapped the file, `map.file_doesnt_need_get = true` and `map.file = desc.vm_file`.
Wait! If `.mmap_prepare` swapped the file, does it *always* give us a new reference?
Yes! The VFS requires that the replaced file has its reference count incremented (or newly allocated) because the original caller (e.g. `ksys_mmap_pgoff`) will eventually drop the reference to the *original* file. So the new mapping MUST take ownership of the new file's reference.
Wait, if it's a NEW file (like `shmem_zero_setup_desc`), then `file != vma->vm_file` for all existing VMAs, so it CANNOT merge.
But what if a driver returns a SINGLETON file?
For example, `cifs_file_mmap_prepare`? Let's check if it swaps the file.",

Wait, only `/dev/zero` via `shmem_zero_setup` swaps the file.
Since `shmem_zero_setup` ALWAYS allocates a NEW file (it calls `__shmem_zero_setup` which calls `alloc_file_pseudo`), the returned file pointer is UNIQUE.
Because the file pointer is unique, `is_mergeable_vma` will ALWAYS evaluate `vma->vm_file != vmg->file` as TRUE, meaning it will ALWAYS return FALSE.
So `vma_merge_new_range` will ALWAYS fail to merge a `/dev/zero` VMA!
Therefore, a `/dev/zero` mapping with a swapped file will NEVER merge with an adjacent VMA.
Thus `allocated_new` will ALWAYS be true!
And `mmap_action_complete()` will ALWAYS be called!
If `mmap_action_complete()` succeeds, `__mmap_region` returns `addr`. The VMA owns the reference.
If `__mmap_new_vma()` fails, `goto unacct_error` -> `abort_munmap` -> `if (map.file_doesnt_need_get) fput(map.file)`. Since `map.file` was updated, the new file is correctly freed.
Wait... so if the file pointer is unique, it never merges, meaning we don't leak it on merge.

```c
}
```

    "content": "squad_1_thought
Wait! `__file_ref_put` has this logic:
```c
	if (WARN_ONCE(cnt >= FILE_REF_RELEASED, "imbalanced put on file reference count")) {
		atomic_long_set(&ref->refcnt, FILE_REF_DEAD);
		return false;
	}
```
If `__file_ref_put` detects an imbalanced put, it returns `false`, which means the caller `fput()` will NOT free the object (because `__file_ref_put` returning false means `file_ref_put` returns false, and `fput` only frees if `file_ref_put` returns true).
BUT it prints a WARNING and leaves the object dead.
Wait! If it returns `false`, the actual double free doesn't happen. The system is protected by `file_ref`!
BUT it is a proven bug (an imbalanced put). A Red Team researcher would STILL report it because it can trigger a kernel warning (`WARN_ONCE`), which is an exploitable denial of service if `panic_on_warn` is set (common in many deployments, like Android or syzkaller itself!).

Wait, is there an actual Use-After-Free?
If `file_ref_put()` was already called inside `__mmap_new_file_vma()`, it transitioned the refcount to `FILE_REF_DEAD`.
When it became `FILE_REF_DEAD`, `fput()` queued it for freeing (`__fput_deferred(file)` -> `schedule_delayed_work`).
So the file is now in the `delayed_fput_list` OR it has already been freed by `delayed_fput_work`.
If it has ALREADY been freed, `fput(map.file)` will dereference a freed pointer when it reads `file->f_ref.refcnt`!
Yes! `fput` does `atomic_long_dec_return(&file->f_ref.refcnt)`. If `file` is freed, this is a memory corruption / Use-After-Free access!
Because `file` is freed asynchronously via `task_work` or `delayed_work`, the race window is large enough, or it could happen immediately if `fput` executes `__fput` synchronously (wait, `fput` defers to `__fput_deferred`, which uses `task_work` if possible).
Since `__mmap_new_vma` returns `-ENOMEM`, `__mmap_region` is still in the same syscall. `task_work` is executed ON RETURN TO USERSPACE.
So the file is NOT freed yet!
Wait, if it's not freed yet, `file` is still allocated.
Then the second `fput(map.file)` happens IN THE SAME SYSCALL.
So the second `fput` will access the STILL-ALLOCATED `file` struct.
It will decrement `f_ref`.
Since `f_ref` was `FILE_REF_DEAD`, it decrements it to `FILE_REF_DEAD - 1`.
`__file_ref_put` sees `cnt >= FILE_REF_RELEASED` (which is like -1073741824) and warns, returning `false`.
So it's a `WARN_ONCE` trigger.
BUT wait! If the attacker calls this thousands of times, the first one triggers `WARN_ONCE`. What about the others?
They silently decrement the refcount further, until it wraps around?
No, it does `atomic_long_set(&ref->refcnt, FILE_REF_DEAD);` to fix it up.
So it doesn't wrap around.
But wait, what about the memory leak if `call_action_prepare()` fails? I proved that `call_action_prepare()` cannot fail for `/dev/zero`, because `MMAP_NOTHING` is used.
Is there ANY other `.mmap_prepare` that CAN fail in `call_action_prepare`?
No, because `/dev/zero` is the ONLY driver in the entire tree that sets `map.file_doesnt_need_get = true` by swapping `desc.vm_file`. And `/dev/zero` uses `MMAP_NOTHING`.

So the ONLY REAL bug here is the imbalanced put / `WARN` trigger (which can be a UAF if `task_work` somehow runs, but it doesn't run in the middle of a syscall unless we sleep and another thread does something? No, `task_work` for `__fput` runs on exit to userspace).
Wait! What if we are using `io_uring`? `io_uring` can execute `mmap`? No, `io_uring` doesn't do `mmap` syscalls directly.
Anyway, an imbalanced put is a severe bug.

Let's summarize the exact steps for the imbalanced put:
1. `ksys_mmap_pgoff(..., MAP_SHARED, fd_zero, 0)` with a large size (triggering `-ENOMEM` in `shmem_inode_acct_blocks`).
2. `mmap_region()` calls `call_mmap_prepare(map, desc)`.
3. `/dev/zero`'s `mmap_zero_prepare()` calls `shmem_zero_setup_desc(desc)`, which allocates a new `shmem` file and sets `desc->vm_file = file;`.
4. `call_mmap_prepare()` sees `desc->vm_file != map->file`, so it sets `map->file_doesnt_need_get = true` and `map->file = desc->vm_file`.
5. `mmap_region()` proceeds to `__mmap_new_vma(map, &vma, &desc.action)`.
6. `__mmap_new_vma()` calls `__mmap_new_file_vma(map, vma)`.
7. `__mmap_new_file_vma()` sets `vma->vm_file = map->file`. Since `map->file_doesnt_need_get` is true, it DOES NOT call `get_file()`.
8. `__mmap_new_file_vma()` calls `mmap_file(vma->vm_file, vma)` which calls `shmem_mmap()`.
9. `shmem_mmap()` calls `shmem_inode_acct_blocks()`, which fails due to the large size, returning `-ENOMEM`.
10. `__mmap_new_file_vma()` catches the error, and executes its error path:
    ```c
    fput(vma->vm_file);
    vma->vm_file = NULL;
    return error;
    ```
    This calls `fput()` on the new shmem file. Its refcount reaches zero, and it is scheduled for freeing (`fput_deferred`).
11. The error `-ENOMEM` propagates back to `__mmap_region()`.
12. `__mmap_region()` jumps to `abort_munmap:`
    ```c
    if (map.file_doesnt_need_get)
        fput(map.file);
    ```
    Because `map.file_doesnt_need_get` is still true, and `map.file` still points to the same shmem file, this calls `fput()` AGAIN on the SAME file!
13. This results in an imbalanced put, triggering a `WARN_ONCE` inside `__file_ref_put_badval()`, and potentially a Use-After-Free if the file struct was already freed by a concurrent or pre-empted task work (though less likely in the exact same syscall thread, but still an invalid memory operation and state corruption).

This is a brilliant finding. It is a textbook double-free/imbalanced put vulnerability introduced by the patch trying to fix a leak. The patch author saw the leak when `vm_area_alloc()` failed (which skips `__mmap_new_file_vma()`), and added `fput(map.file)` at the very end. But they failed to realize that if the error happens INSIDE `__mmap_new_file_vma()`, that function ALREADY cleans up the file by calling `fput(vma->vm_file)`.

Is there any other case where `__mmap_new_file_vma()` is skipped but `__mmap_new_vma()` fails?
Yes, `vma_start_write_killable(vma)` and `vma_iter_prealloc(vmi, vma)`. If they fail, `__mmap_new_file_vma()` is skipped, and the patch's `fput` correctly frees the file.
So the correct fix would have been to set `map.file = NULL` or `map.file_doesnt_need_get = false` inside `__mmap_new_file_vma()`'s error path, or similar. But since the patch just blindly added `fput(map.file)` at the end, it causes a double free.

Let's double check if `map.file` is modified inside `__mmap_new_file_vma()` on error.
```c
	error = mmap_file(vma->vm_file, vma);
	if (error) {
		UNMAP_STATE(unmap, vmi, vma, vma->vm_start, vma->vm_end,
			    map->prev, map->next);
		fput(vma->vm_file);
		vma->vm_file = NULL;

		vma_iter_set(vmi, vma->vm_end);
		/* Undo any partial mapping done by a device driver. */
		unmap_region(&unmap);
		return error;
	}
```
No, `map->file` is NOT modified here. Only `vma->vm_file` is set to NULL.
So `map->file` is left pointing to the `fput()`ted file.
And `abort_munmap` uses `map.file`.

Perfect! The reasoning is absolutely solid.

Let's write down the concern clearly.

Concern:
- type: "Double Free / Imbalanced Put"
- description: "The patch introduces a double free (imbalanced put) of `map.file` when `mmap_file()` fails."
- reasoning: "When a driver like `/dev/zero` swaps the file in `.mmap_prepare()`, `map.file_doesnt_need_get` is set to true and `map.file` points to the new file with a reference count of 1. If `__mmap_new_vma()` fails inside `__mmap_new_file_vma()` because `mmap_file()` (e.g., `shmem_mmap()`) returns an error (like `-ENOMEM`), `__mmap_new_file_vma()` cleans up by calling `fput(vma->vm_file)` which drops the reference count to 0. It then returns the error to `__mmap_region()`. The newly added code at `abort_munmap` sees `map.file_doesnt_need_get` is true and calls `fput(map.file)` again on the same file pointer. This results in a double free / imbalanced put, which triggers a kernel `WARN_ONCE` and causes memory state corruption."

Are there any other concerns?
What about the memory leak on merge success?
Earlier I thought if `vma_merge_new_range` succeeds, it leaks the file reference.
Let's trace that.
Can a `/dev/zero` mapping (the only one that swaps the file) merge with an adjacent VMA?
I reasoned that `shmem_zero_setup_desc()` allocates a NEW file every time.
Because it's a new `struct file`, `is_mergeable_vma()` will see `vma->vm_file != vmg->file` and return false.
Therefore, `vma_merge_new_range()` will ALWAYS fail for these mappings.
So they will NEVER merge.
Thus, the memory leak on merge success cannot be triggered for `/dev/zero`.
Is it possible for another driver to return a singleton file?
As I checked, no other driver in the tree uses `.mmap_prepare` to swap files.
So the merge memory leak is a theoretical API trap but not currently reachable in the tree.
Wait, if it is a theoretical trap, it's still a flaw in the API contract.
Let's describe it as a secondary concern.

Secondary Concern:
- type: "Memory Leak"
- description: "If a driver ever swaps `desc.vm_file` with a file that can merge with an adjacent VMA, the reference to the new file is leaked on merge."
- reasoning: "If `call_mmap_prepare()` sets `map.file_doesnt_need_get = true` and transfers ownership of the file reference to `map`, but `__mmap_region()` successfully merges the mapping with an adjacent VMA via `vma_merge_new_range()`, `allocated_new` becomes false. In this success path, `__mmap_region()` returns `addr` without dropping the extra reference to `map.file`. The adjacent VMA already holds its own reference to the file, and the new reference acquired from `.mmap_prepare()` is permanently leaked."

Wait, what if `allocated_new` is false, does `__mmap_complete` `fput` it? No.
So this is a valid theoretical concern.

Any other concerns?
What about the leak when `call_action_prepare()` fails?
Since `/dev/zero` leaves `desc->action.type = MMAP_NOTHING`, `call_action_prepare()` returns 0 and cannot fail.
If another driver used `.mmap_prepare` to swap the file AND set `desc->action.type = MMAP_REMAP_PFN` (for example) and that failed, `call_mmap_prepare()` would return an error WITHOUT updating `map.file_doesnt_need_get`. This would leak the new file allocated in `vfs_mmap_prepare()`.
Also a theoretical API trap.

Let's stick to the double-free as the absolute primary concern because it is 100% reachable with existing drivers (`/dev/zero`).

Wait, is the new file cleaned up if `mmap_action_complete` fails?
As I analyzed earlier, `mmap_action_complete` calls `do_munmap`, which unmaps the VMA. Unmapping the VMA calls `fput(vma->vm_file)`. So the new file IS properly cleaned up.

If `__mmap_region` returns `error` directly, the original file is cleaned up by the caller (`ksys_mmap_pgoff`). So both files are cleaned up properly.
So NO bug there.

If `!map->file_doesnt_need_get`, it gets the file.
Since for swapped files `map->file_doesnt_need_get` is true, it skips `get_file()`.
So `vma->vm_file` has exactly 1 reference (the one owned by `map->file`).
Then `mmap_file()` fails.
`fput(vma->vm_file)` is called. Refcount drops to 0. File is scheduled for freeing.
Then `return error;`.
`__mmap_new_vma()` returns error to `__mmap_region()`.
`__mmap_region()` jumps to `abort_munmap`.
`abort_munmap` calls `fput(map.file)`.
Since `map.file` was not updated to NULL, it points to the freed file.

    "content": "# Stage 7. Hardware engineer's review

You are a hardware engineer reviewing device driver changes. If this patch touches driver or hardware-specific code, rigorously review register accesses, IRQ handling, DMA mapping/unmapping, memory barriers, and timing/delays. Look for missing dma_wmb()/dma_rmb() barriers, incorrect endianness conversions (cpu_to_le32), and unsafe DMA buffer allocations. Ensure the hardware state machine is handled correctly, especially during suspend/resume or device reset. Evaluate the physical state machine constraints: verify that clocks and power domains are enabled before registers are accessed, and that hardware rings/queues are actually initialized in the current hardware state before being unconditionally accessed. If the patch is purely generic software logic (e.g., VFS, core networking), output an empty concerns list.



Once you have gathered sufficient information, return ONLY a JSON object with a "concerns" array.
If you find no concerns, return `{"concerns": []}`.
If you find concerns, each must be an object with:
- "type": A short category string.
- "description": A clear description of the problem.
- "reasoning": A step-by-step explanation.

CRITICAL REVIEW DIRECTIVE: Do NOT dismiss concerns just because you assume the surrounding system or caller handles it perfectly. Do not be overly charitable to the existing code. If there is a missing initialization, an unhandled edge case, or a brittle logic flow, report it as a concern immediately. Assume the worst-case scenario where external inputs and caller states are malformed.

    "content": "# Stage 8. Verification and severity estimation

You are the lead reviewer consolidating feedback from multiple specialized analysts. You will be given a list of concerns generated by different review stages.
1. Deduplicate identical or overlapping concerns.
2. Validate each concern and prove the provided reasoning. Report all valid concerns as findings. If necessarily, use tools to gather additional material. Discard all false positives
3. CRITICAL RULE: To discard a concern as a false positive, you MUST find concrete proof that explicitly invalidates the concern's reasoning. If you cannot find definitive proof that the concern is a false positive, it must be reported as a finding. If you're not sure about something and it's critical in the reasoning validation, make it obvious: if X is possible, then problem Y can occur. Always try to validate if X is possible yourself.
4. If context from subsequent patches in the series is provided, check if the concern is fixed later in the series. If so, discard it. But don't trust any promises in the commit message if they can't be verified (e.g. something will be fixed by subsequent patches in the series - if you can't prove that it's indeed fixed, report it as a bug).
5. When referring to other patches within this series in your explanation, DO NOT use git hashes (they are ephemeral/unstable). Instead, refer to them by their patch subject (e.g., 'commit "mm: fix allocation"'). Existing historical commits in the tree should still be referenced by their standard hash.
6. Assign a severity (low, medium, high, critical) to each remaining valid finding and explain the reasoning. Be rigorous in filtering out verifiable noise, but accurately report real logic flaws and edge cases.
7. If the problem did exist in the code before the patch was applied, say it explicitly: 'This problem wasn't introduced by this patch, but...'. Discard low- and medium-severity pre-existing problems, report only high- and critical severity issues.

However, `map.file_doesnt_need_get` and `map.file` are only updated at the very end of `call_mmap_prepare()`, after `call_action_prepare()` succeeds.\n\nIf `vfs_mmap_prepare()` successfully swaps the file (updating `desc->vm_file` with a new file that has an elevated refcount) but the subsequent `call_action_prepare()` fails, `call_mmap_prepare()` will return an error early. Because `map.file_doesnt_need_get` is false, the `fput()` is skipped, and the newly allocated file stored in the local `desc.vm_file` variable is permanently leaked when `desc` goes out of scope.\n\nA robust fix should directly check if the descriptor's file differs from the original file (e.g., `if (desc.vm_file && desc.vm_file != file) fput(desc.vm_file);`) rather than relying on `map.file_doesnt_need_get`, which is not immediately updated.",
